Akahama and Kawamura 2004.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Akahama and Kawamura 2004
category: 1
composition: Diamond
method: Raman
technique: DAC
DAC_type: 
culet: 50-400 #um
cal_to_name: Mao et al., 1986; Holmes et al., 1989; Vohra et al., 1987
cal_to_composition: Al2O3; Pt; Re
eos: Akahama04_Equation
V0: 1332.5
V0_unc: 1 
A: 66.9
A_unc: 0.7
B: -0.5281
B_unc: 0.0004
C: 0.0003585
C_unc: 0.0000003
D: 736
D_unc: 1 
E: 52.81
E_unc: 0.07
F: 127.5
F_unc: 0.8
PTM: None
max_pressure: 250 #GPa
synchrotron: BL10XU at SPring-8
doi: 10.1063/1.1778482
data_quality_notes: |
    The standard deviation of the pressure calibration is ~0.66 GPa, increasing to ~1.27 GPa in the multimegabar (>100 GPa) regime.
notes: |
    This study used ruby to ~100 GPa and Pt above 100 GPa. They repot two equations. 
#P(GPa)=A-B*v+C*v^2   and
#v(cm-1)=D+E*(P+F)^(1/2)
Akahama and Kawamura 2006.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Akahama and Kawamura 2006
category: 1
composition: Diamond

technique:
DAC_type: 
culet: 30-35 #um

cal_to_composition: Pt 
eos: Akahama06_Equation
V0: 1334
A: 547 #K0
A_unc: 11
B: 3.75 #K0p
B_unc: 0.20
PTM: None
max_pressure: 310 #GPa
synchrotron: BL10XU at SPring-8
doi: doi.org/10.1063/1.2335683
data_quality_notes: |
    The average pressure uncertainty derived from Pt EOS is ±2 GPa below 250 GPa, increasing to ±3 GPa at 310 GPa due to deviatoric stress in the pressure marker.

    They repot 1 equation.
# pressure=K0*((V-V0)/V0)*(1+0.5*(K0p-1)*((V-V0)/V0))
Akahama and Kawamura 2010.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Akahama and Kawamura 2010
category: 1
composition: Diamond
method: Raman
technique: DAC
DAC_type: 
culet: double bevel with first bevel at 250 um and 8 degrees; Second bevel at 450 um and 15 degrees
cal_to_name: Holmes et al., 1989
cal_to_composition: Pt 
eos: Akahama10_Equation
V0: 1334
A: 547 #K0
A_unc: 11
B: 3.75 #K0p
B_unc: 0.20
C: 3141
C_unc: 3
D: 4.157
D_unc: 0.020
E: 0.001429
E_unc: 0.000012
PTM: None
max_pressure: 410 #GPa
synchrotron: BLX10XU at SPring-8
doi: 10.1088/1742-6596/215/1/012195
notes: |
    They repot 2 equation. The high-frequency edge corresponds to the singlet mode of the stress-split F₂g phonon for [001]-oriented anvils and shifts monotonically with increasing pressure.
# pressure=K0*((V-V0)/V0)*(1+0.5*(K0p-1)*((V-V0)/V0)) below 200 GPa (aka: Akahama06_equation)
# pressure=C-D*v+E*v^2
Akahama et al., 2002_Au.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Akahama et al., 2002
category: 1
composition: Au  # bcc phase-5
method: XRD
technique: DAC
DAC_type: symmetric DAC
culet: 150; 50  # um (two experimental runs)
cal_to_name: Holmes et al., 1989
cal_to_composition: Pt
eos: Rydberg-Vinet
order: 3
V0_per_atom: 16.9671  # Å^3
V0_unc: 0.0066
K0: 166.34  # GPa
is_K0_fixed: no
K0_unc: 0.77
K0_prime: 6.244
K0_prime_unc: 0.033
max_pressure: 145  # GPa
PTM: none
gasket: Re
synchrotron: SPring-8 BL04B2
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: 110; 200; 211; 220
reference_temperature: 300 K
sample_heating: none
data_quality_notes: |
  Unit-cell volumes of Bi, Pt, and Au were measured simultaneously by
  powder XRD. Pressure was determined from the Pt EOS. The bcc phase of
  Bi was observed to be stable up to the maximum pressure of 222 GPa.
  Vinet and Birch–Murnaghan EOS were both tested; the Vinet EOS provided
  a marginally better fit based on rms residuals.
doi: 10.1063/1.1515378
notes: |
  This study provides a primary static EOS for bcc Bi at 300 K to
  megabar pressures. The atomic volume V0 was determined by iterative
  fitting to minimize rms residuals, not taken from ambient-pressure
  crystallography.

  Although Au and Pt compression data are discussed, the Au EOS values
  reported here are explicitly tied to the Pt pressure scale and are
  secondary to the main goal of calibrating Bi.

  The authors demonstrate that the Au pressure scale underestimates
  pressure relative to Pt above ~30 GPa, diverging by ~20 GPa at
  145 GPa.

Akahama et al., 2004_Ag.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Akahama et al., 2004
category: 2
composition: Ag
method: XRD
technique: DAC
DAC_type: Beveled diamond-anvil cell
culet: 300 μm (150 μm anvil face, 8.5° bevel angle)

cal_to_name: Anderson et al., 1989
cal_to_composition: Au
cal_to_eos: Birch-Murnaghan
eos: Rydberg-Vinet
order: 3

V0_per_atom: 17.057     #assumed from Table 2, Carter et al., 
V0_unc:

K0: 89.8                 # GPa 
K0_unc: 1.3
is_K0_fixed: no
K0_prime: 5.92
K0_prime_unc: 0.07

max_pressure: 150
PTM: None   # no pressure medium used
gasket: Re
synchrotron: SPring-8 BL04B2
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: fcc Ag reflections (5–8 peaks used per pattern)

reference_temperature: 300
sample_heating: none

data_quality_notes: >
  Ag and Au samples were simultaneously compressed to ~150 GPa with no PTM,
  leading to measurable but relatively small differential stresses. Nonhydrostatic
  stress correction was applied using measured at-values obtained from gamma-plots.
  A Vinet EOS fit to corrected Ag compression data yields K0 = 89.8(1.3) GPa and
  K0' = 5.92(7). Constraining K0 to the ultrasonic value of 98(1.6) GPa gives K0' = 5.47(2).
  Volume correction uncertainties produce up to ±4 GPa pressure uncertainty for Ag
  at 150 GPa.

doi: 10.1063/1.1690456

notes: >
  The published Ag EOS is one of the few high-pressure Ag calibrations extending to 150 GPa. Both corrected and uncorrected datasets show consistent trends. K0 = 89.8 GPa is preferred for direct comparison within this study, while K0 = 98 GPa (ultrasonic anchored) provides consistency with ultrasonic-based standards.

Akahama et al., 2006_Al_BM_k0fixed.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Akahama et al., 2006
category: 1
composition: Al #fcc
method: XRD
technique: DAC
DAC_type: 
culet: 30-50  #um
cal_to_name: Akahama and Kawamura 2004; Holmes et al., 1989 #separate by a ; if multiple references are used 
cal_to_composition: Diamond; Pt  #separate by a ; if multiple compositions are used  

eos: Birch-Murnaghan
order: 3
V0: 66.39
V0_unc: 0.002
K0: 72.7  #GPa, ultrasonics 
is_K0_fixed: yes
K0_unc:
K0_prime: 4.446
K0_prime_unc: 0.087
max_pressure: 222   # GPa; 333 highest pressure reached in hcp field, fcc EOS constrained up to transition region
PTM: None #(Al + Pt in Re gasket; Al acts as soft medium)
gasket: Re
synchrotron: SPring-8 BL10XU
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: fcc 111, 200, 220, 311 (EOS fit); hcp 100, 101, 102 near transition
reference_temperature: 297 K
sample_heating: none

data_quality_notes: |
  Pressures were determined from the EOS of Pt (Holmes et al., 1989) using in situ Pt lattice parameters.
  No pressure transmitting medium was used; however, Al itself (lower shear modulus) acted as an efficient
  pressure medium, limiting uniaxial stress. Standard deviation of Pt-derived pressures is ~0.6 GPa for the
  preferred Vinet fit with K0 fixed at 72.7 GPa and K0' = 4.83. The fcc–hcp transition begins at V/V0 = 0.509(1),
  corresponding to 217 ± 10 GPa.

doi: 10.1103/PhysRevLett.96.045505
notes: |
  EOS parameters are for fcc-Al at 297 K, fitted to the Vinet formulation with K0 fixed to the ultrasonic/
  low-pressure value 72.7 GPa and K0' = 4.83(3). This EOS is proposed as suitable for use as a pressure
  standard up to the multimegabar range. Above ~217 GPa, hcp-Al appears and coexists with fcc-Al over a
  broad pressure interval; the EOS here describes the fcc branch only.

Akahama et al., 2014_Mo.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Akahama et al., 2014
category: 1
composition: Mo
method: XRD
technique: DAC
DAC_type: 
culet: 25 μm (Run 1), 20 μm (Run 2); with 250 μm and 450/350 μm bevels  #um
cal_to_name: Holmes et al., 1989  #separate by a ; if multiple references are used 
cal_to_composition: Pt  #separate by a ; if multiple compositions are used  
eos: Rydberg-Vinet
order: 3
V0: 31.155
V0_unc: 0.024
K0: 262.3  #GPa
is_K0_fixed: no
K0_unc: 4.6
K0_prime: 4.55
K0_prime_unc: 0.16
max_pressure: 410        # GPa; highest pressure achieved in the study 
PTM: None #(Mo + Pt embedded directly in Re gasket)
gasket: Re
synchrotron: SPring-8 BL10XU
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: Mo (200) for volume; Pt (111) for pressure  # described on p. 3 
reference_temperature: 298 K
sample_heating: none

data_quality_notes: |
  Unit-cell volumes of Mo and Pt were measured simultaneously. Pressure is taken
  from the Pt Vinet EOS. The Mo lattice constants were extracted primarily from
  the (200) reflection to minimize nonhydrostatic effects, which remained small.
  Standard deviation in Mo lattice parameters ranged from 0.0005–0.0036 Å.
  Uniaxial stress analysis (Fig. 5 and 6) shows limited nonhydrostatic distortion
  thanks to the Re gasket confinement.

doi: 10.1063/1.4903940

notes: |
  The bcc phase of Mo remained stable up to 410 GPa.
  The EOS agrees with ultrasonic data to within 2.5% at 200 GPa and 3.3% at 400 GPa,
  but differs significantly from shock-derived EOSs (7–11% lower pressures).
  The authors conclude the Pt pressure scale is reliable in the multimegabar regime.
Aleksandrov et al., 1987_Al2O3.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Aleksandrov et al., 1987
category: 1
composition: Al2O3  # ruby (Cr-doped α-Al2O3)
method: Luminescence
technique: DAC
DAC_type: not specified
culet: not specified
cal_to_name: Piermarini et al., 1975; Mao et al., 1978; Jayaraman, 1986
cal_to_composition: Al2O3; Al2O3; Al2O3
eos: Polynomial
order: 2
A: 1892   # GPa
A_unc: 13
B: 6.4
B_unc: 0.5
lambda_0: 694.25  # nm
max_pressure: 42  # GPa
PTM: He
reference_temperature: 297
PTM: He
data_quality_notes: |
  Pressure determination relies on the ruby R1 fluorescence scale, which the
  authors identify as internally inconsistent at high pressures. Above ~35–40 GPa,
  deviation from hydrostatic conditions is observed due to helium solidification, leading to
  crystal degradation (rocking-curve broadening) and Raman line broadening. X-ray lattice
  parameter measurements are limited to a single (440) reflection and are affected by non-
  hydrostatic stress. Absolute pressure values above ~40 GPa are model-dependent and tied to
  an a prior diamond EOS rather than an independently validated pressure scale. 

notes: |
  Aleksandrov et al. (1987) combine Raman scattering and x-ray diffraction to derive the diamond
  equation of state up to ~42 GPa and directly determine the Grüneisen parameter for the LTO(T)
  mode (γ₀ ≈ 0.965), finding it density-independent up to ~40 GPa. The work demonstrates that
  discrepancies between experimental EOS data, ultrasonic measurements, and theory arise
  primarily from systematic errors in the ruby pressure scale, and proposes a revised high-
  pressure calibration based on an a priori diamond EOS. 
doi: Diamond at high pressures Raman scattering of light, equation of state, and high-pressure scale.

A: 442 #K0
A_unc:
B: 4.0 #K0p
B_unc: 0.7
max_pressure: 42 #GPa
PTM: He
reference_temperature: Room temperature #297 K
doi: | 
    Title: Diamond at high pressures: Raman scattering of light, equation of state, and high pressure scale. 

data_quality_notes: |
  Pressure determination relies on the contemporaneous ruby R1 fluorescence scale, which the
  authors explicitly identify as internally inconsistent at high pressures. Above ~35–40 GPa,
  deviation from hydrostatic conditions is observed due to helium solidification, leading to
  crystal degradation (rocking-curve broadening) and Raman line broadening. X-ray lattice
  parameter measurements are limited to a single (440) reflection and are affected by non-
  hydrostatic stress. Absolute pressure values above ~40 GPa are model-dependent and tied to
  an a priori diamond EOS rather than an independently validated pressure scale. 
notes: |
  Aleksandrov et al. (1987) combine Raman scattering and x-ray diffraction to derive the diamond
  equation of state up to ~42 GPa and directly determine the Grüneisen parameter for the LTO(T)
  mode (γ₀ ≈ 0.965), finding it density-independent up to ~40 GPa. The work demonstrates that
  discrepancies between experimental EOS data, ultrasonic measurements, and theory arise
  primarily from systematic errors in the ruby pressure scale, and proposes a revised high-
  pressure calibration based on an a priori diamond EOS. 

# Raman–EOS relation from the paper:
# 1) Grüneisen-type relation:  (ν/ν0) = (V/V0)^(-γ)
#    where γ = 0.965 ± 0.005
# 2) Density–Raman relation:  ρ/ρ0 - 1 = 4.28(ν/ν0 - 1)
# 3) Pressure–Raman polynomial expansion:
#       P = K0 * (Δν/ν0) + 0.5 * K0 * (K0' - 1) * (Δν/ν0)^2
#    where K0 = 442 GPa, K0' = 4.0, Δν = ν - ν

study: Anderson et al., 1989

K0: 166.65  # GPa (KT0 at 300 K)
is_K0_fixed: no
K0_prime: 5.4823

max_pressure: not specified #(model EOS)
PTM: not applicable
reference_temperature: 300 K
sample_heating: none
data_quality_notes: |
  This study does not report new static compression data.
  Instead, it derives a thermodynamically consistent equation
  of state for gold by combining ultrasonic elastic constants,
  thermal expansivity, and specific heat data.

  The isothermal bulk modulus KT0 at 300 K is obtained from
  elastic constants using standard thermodynamic relations.
  Pressure derivatives are constrained by consistency
  between (∂KT/∂P)T, thermal pressure, and anharmonic terms.
doi: 10.1063/1.342969
notes: |
  The authors argue that thermodynamic consistency favors
  (∂KT/∂P)T ≈ 5.5 at ambient conditions, lower than some
  earlier values. This choice leads to a nonzero
  (∂KT/∂T)V and significant anharmonic corrections to the
  thermal pressure at high temperature.

  The EOS is expressed as:
    P(V,T) = P_BM(V,300) + P_TH(V,T),
  where P_BM is a third-order Birch–Murnaghan isotherm
  and P_TH includes explicit anharmonic volume dependence.

  This YAML represents the **reference 300 K isotherm only**.
  The full thermal EOS (anharmonic Mie–Grüneisen–type
  formulation) should be stored as a separate linked entry.

  (2) fixed V0 = 32.23 Å^3 (from DFT), giving K0 = 34.0 ± 0.2 GPa and
      K0' = 6.0 ± 0.02.---called b
  Both fits yield nearly identical P–V curves above 10 GPa.

  Pressure determination uses Cu (Runs A, B) and Ne (Run C), with Ne
  calibrated internally to Cu to minimize pressure-scale chaining.
  This study supersedes Akahama et al. (2002) by extending the pressure
  range to ~260 GPa under softer, better-controlled stress conditions.

sample_heating: double-sided laser heating (up to ~2400 K)
data_quality_notes: |
  The 300 K isothermal EOS of B2-KCl was obtained by fitting
  room-temperature data from Dewaele et al. (2012) together
  with new high-P data collected prior to heating. A third-order
  Birch–Murnaghan EOS was found to better describe the
  low-pressure B2 data than a Rydberg–Vinet form.

  The 200 reflection of KCl shows strong sensitivity to
  nonhydrostatic strain and was excluded above ~45 GPa;
  above ~130 GPa it is no longer observable.
doi: [https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevB.104.094107]
notes: |
  This YAML represents the reference 300 K EOS used as the
  baseline for the full Mie–Grüneisen–Debye thermal EOS
  developed in this study.

  The authors also report an alternative Rydberg–Vinet
  reference EOS (V0 = 34.3(5) cm^3/mol, K0 = 13(1) GPa,
  K0' = 6.2(1)), but favor the Birch–Murnaghan form because
  it better captures the B1–B2 transition behavior and yields
  lower systematic residuals at low pressure.

  Thermal parameters reported separately are:
  γ0 = 2.9(4), q = 1.0(1), θD = 235 K.

Cynn and Yoo 1998_Ta.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Cynn and Yoo 1999
category: 1
composition: Ta
method: XRD
technique: DAC
DAC_type: Mao-Bell
culet: 300 μm (low P), beveled 300→100 μm with 7° bevel (high P)
cal_to_name: Heinz and Jeanloz 1984
cal_to_composition: Au

eos: Birch-Murnaghan
order: 3
V0_cc_mole: 10.865
V0_unc:
K0: 194.7  #GPa
is_K0_fixed: no
K0_unc: 4.8
K0_prime: 3.4
K0_prime_unc: 0.1

K0_dprime:
K0_dprime_unc:

max_pressure: 174
PTM: Ar
gasket: Re
synchrotron: SSRL beamline 10-2
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: Seven bcc-Ta reflections (110, 200, 211, etc.)

reference_temperature: 300    # K
sample_heating: none

data_quality_notes: >
  Angle-resolved synchrotron XRD was used to measure the compression of bcc-Ta
  up to 174 GPa. Pressures were determined using an internal Au calibrant with
  the Heinz & Jeanloz (1984) 3rd-order Birch–Murnaghan EOS (V0 = 10.215 cm^3/mol,
  K0T = 166.6 GPa, K' = 5.5). Two DAC configurations were used: 300-μm flats for
  pressures to 50 GPa and beveled anvils (300→100 μm, 7°) for higher pressures.
  Pressure uncertainties were 0.7–2% using ruby and ~1.5% from the Au EOS.
  The measured lattice parameters show no evidence of structural transitions,
  confirming that bcc Ta is stable to 174 GPa. The derived EOS parameters
  (K0T = 194.7±4.8 GPa, K' = 3.4±0.1) agree with ultrasonic B0S measurements
  converted to isothermal conditions (189–195 GPa). Earlier XRD results (Xu et al.)
  overestimated K0T due to a rapidly softening EOS fit.

doi: 10.1103/PhysRevB.59.8526

notes: >
  This EOS is widely used as a high-pressure standard. The relatively low K'
  (compared to Au) means Ta compresses more uniformly with pressure and provides
  more stable pressure determination above 100 GPa. All EOS parameters correspond
  to isothermal values at ambient temperature.
Datchi and Canny 2004_cBN_M1.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Datchi and Canny 2004 (M1) #Model 1
category: 1
composition: cBN
method: Raman
technique: DAC (Raman spectroscopy at simultaneous high P–T)
DAC_type: membrane DAC (MDAC)
culet: not specified

# --- Pressure determination ---
# Ruby and SrB4O7:Sm2+ explicitly loaded and equations given
cal_to_name: Mao et al., 1986; Datchi et al., 1997
cal_to_composition: Al2O3; SrB4O7


eos: RamanDatchi04

# --- Full P–T Raman calibration (Eq. 8, model 2 preferred) ---
# ν_TO(P,T) = ν0 + aT + bT^2 + (c0 + c1 T + c2 T^2) P + d P^2
V0: 1058.4                  # cm^-1
V0_unc: 0.2                # cm^-1 
A: -0.0096                   # cm^-1/K
A_unc: 0.0007            # cm^-1/K
B: -0.0000154                # cm^-1/K^2
B_unc: 0.000007                # cm^-1/K^2
C: 3.325                     # cm^-1/GPa; C0
C_unc: 0.011                 # cm^-1/GPa
D: 0.00022                  # cm^-1/(GPa·K)
D_unc: 0.000015              # cm^-1/(GPa·K)
E: 0
F: -0.0115
F_unc: 0.0004

max_pressure: 21             # GPa
PTM: Ne 
gasket: Re
synchrotron: none
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: c-BN TO Raman mode
reference_temperature: 300–723   # K
sample_heating: resistive external heating

data_quality_notes: |
  Raman measurements performed on a single c-BN crystal compressed
  in neon. Pressure measured in situ using ruby and SrB4O7:Sm2+
  fluorescence; explicit wavelength–pressure equations are given
  in the text (Eqs. 1–2). The TO-mode frequency shift is shown to be
  a coupled function of pressure and temperature.

  Bulk modulus and its pressure derivative are derived quantities,
  obtained by inverting a first-order Murnaghan EOS using a literature
  mode Grüneisen parameter; they are not independently calibrated EOS
  parameters.

doi: 10.1103/PhysRevB.69.144106
notes: |
  This study provides the first experimental Raman calibration of c-BN
  at simultaneous high pressure and temperature (to 21 GPa, 723 K).
  The preferred pressure-reading formulation is given by inversion of
  Eq. (8) (model 2), yielding pressure uncertainties of ~0.15 GPa
  within the calibrated range.

  The Raman-based pressure scale is intended for high-T DAC experiments
  where ruby becomes unreliable; extrapolation beyond the measured P–T
  range is discouraged.

Datchi et al., 1997_SrB4O7.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Datchi et al., 1997
category: 1
composition: SrB4O7 #Sm2+
method: Luminescence
technique: DAC
DAC_type: membrane DAC (MDAC)
culet: not specified
cal_to_name: Mao et al., 1986
cal_to_composition: Al2O3
eos: Datchi97_Equation  #Pressure=A*Δλ (1+BΔλ)/(1+CΔλ)
A: 4.032
A_unc:
B: 0.00929 
B_unc: 0.0004
C: 0.0232 
C_unc: 0.0006
lambda_0: 685.41  # nm
max_pressure: 124  # GPa hydrostatic, nonhydrostatic to 130 GPa
PTM: He
gasket: Re
synchrotron: ESRF ID9
peaks_used_to_fit_sample:
reference_temperature: 300 #K
sample_heating: 
data_quality_notes: |
    Overall, uncertainties are mainly governed by the ruby primary scale 
    (~3% at 100 GPa) and gold EOS (~1–2%), while spectroscopic precision is high. 
    Datchi et al. (1997) show that the SrB₄O₇:Sm²⁺ (0–0) fluorescence line has 
    extremely low temperature sensitivity, with negligible shift up to 500 K and 
    only a very small nonlinear shift (~0.06 nm) up to 900 K—far smaller than 
    ruby’s temperature dependence. Thermal broadening remains limited, though 
    signal intensity decreases significantly above 700 K, restricting practical 
    use to about 900 K. The study also provides an updated calibration of ruby’s 
    R₁ temperature shift, linear up to 600 K and nonlinear at higher temperatures.
    The authors propose an improved in situ pressure–temperature metrology method
    for diamond anvil cells. Overall accuracy is mainly limited by the ruby 
    pressure scale and gold equation of state.
doi: [https://doi.org/10.1063/1.365025]
notes: Additonal experiment was done in ICE PTM, no equation was derived, this experiment was mixed with Au and Al2O3, XRD was collected at the ESRF. They show good agreement Fig 3. Datchi et al. (1997) recalibrated the SrB₄O₇:Sm²⁺ (0–0) fluorescence line as an optical pressure gauge up to 124 GPa in helium and 130 GPa in ice, with temperature calibration up to 900 K. The study demonstrates strong data quality, based on 58 well-reproduced pressure measurements with minimal scatter. The pressure-induced wavelength shift is nonlinear and accurately described by a quadratic fit, while an alternative Padé-type equation reproduces pressures with a mean deviation of only 0.1 GPa. Validation against the gold equation of state shows excellent agreement (within 1.4% in helium and 2.6% in ice), confirming calibration reliability. Unlike ruby, the borate 0–0 line remains sharp, intense, and well isolated even at very high pressures, with no luminescence quenching observed up to 130 GPa, making it a more robust and accurate high-pressure sensor.

study: Datchi et al., 2007

eos: Raman_Datchi
V0: 1054          # cm^-1
V0_unc: 0.1        # cm^-1
A: 396.5 # labeled as B0 in text
A_unc: 0.5
max_pressure: 100     # GPa (recommended validity range for the Raman scale)
PTM: He; Ne (for EOS constraints); Raman data compared to earlier HP–HT work
gasket: Re
synchrotron: none (Raman)
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: c-BN TO Raman mode (~1054 cm^-1 at ambient)
reference_temperature: 300-1000  # K (intended use range)
sample_heating: resistive heating (for HP–HT constraint/calibration)
data_quality_notes: |
  This Raman TO-mode pressure scale is “constrained” by independently measured physical parameters:
  B0(T) derived from the study’s P–V–T EOS and B0' held constant; ω0_TO(T) allowed within bounds of prior
  measurements and refined here. Authors state it can be safely used over ~0–100 GPa and 300–1000 K.
doi: 10.1103/PhysRevB.75.214104
notes: |
  Constrained Raman pressure scale (Eq. 8):
    P = (B0(T)/3.62) * [ ( ω_TO(P,T) / ω0_TO(T) )^(2.876) - 1 ].
  B0(T) (Eq. 7):
    B0(T>=300K) = 396.5(5) - 0.0288(14)*(T-300) - 6.84(77)e-6*(T-300)^2  [GPa].
  ω0_TO(T) (given with Eq. 8):
    ω0_TO(T) = 1058.4(5) - 0.0091(23)*T - 1.54(22)e-5*T^2  [cm-1].

method: XRD #Calculated EOS
technique: Mie–Gruneisen–Debye formulation
DAC_type: N/A
culet: N/A

order: 3

P0: 0
reference_temperature: 25 #C

# Elastic parameters (Table I)
K0: 25.03                   # GPa
is_K0_fixed: yes # K0 held fixed during fitting
K0_unc: 0.08                # GPa
K0_prime: 4.5206
K0_prime_unc: 0.00508

max_pressure: 30       
PTM: N/A
synchrotron: N/A
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: N/A
sample_heating: modeled up to 2000 °C

data_quality_notes: |
  Pressure is calculated using a Mie–Gruneisen–Debye EOS constrained
  by experimentally determined elastic constants, thermal expansion,
  Debye temperature, and Gruneisen parameter. The paper provides
  tabulated pressure values as a function of compression and
  temperature; no closed-form pressure–volume equation is proposed.

  Estimated pressure uncertainty is stated to increase with pressure,
  from approximately 1.1% below 50 kbar to ~2.4% below 200 kbar.

  Maoie–Gruneisen–Debye EOS is not supported in EoSAlign; so we have refit using BM for direct comparison.

doi: 10.1063/1.1660714
notes: |
  This study presents a calculated P–V–T equation of state for NaCl.
  All pressure values are derived from the model described in the text
  and tables. The EOS is intended for interpolation within the tabulated
  pressure–volume–temperature range and should not be extrapolated
  beyond the limits discussed by the author.

  of amorphization or irreversible changes. Peak broadening above ~80 GPa
  attributed to nonhydrostaticity. Luminescence shifts on decompression are minor.
doi: https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevB.88.064107
notes: |
  Experimental Rydberg-Vinet fit parameters were obtained from three runs using
  He PTM. Fit values match well with ultrasonic bulk modulus (253 ± 2 GPa).
  Pressure calibration relied on ruby luminescence. Authors caution against
  extrapolating beyond 165 GPa.
Dewaele et al., 2000_MgO.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Dewaele et al., 2000
category: 1
composition: MgO  # Periclase (B1)
method: XRD
technique: DAC (laser-heated diamond anvil cell)
DAC_type:
culet: ~300 µm  # (diameter not explicitly stated; typical for Chervin-type; left approximate)
cal_to_name: Not Specified #analysis same as Jamieson et al., 1982
cal_to_composition: Pt
eos: Birch-Murnaghan
order: 3
V0: 74.71   # Å^3 per formula unit (MgO), fixed (page 2873)
V0_unc: 0.03
K0: 161    # GPa (fixed) (page 2873)
is_K0_fixed: yes
K0_unc: 1  # GPa (acoustic constraint cited in study)
K0_prime: 3.94
K0_prime_unc: 0.2
max_pressure: 53   # GPa (measured up to 53 GPa and 2474 K)
PTM: Ar #+ minor N2 (cryogenic loading; quasi-hydrostatic)  # page 2871–2872
synchrotron: ESRF ID30 (European Synchrotron Radiation Facility, Grenoble)
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: MgO (Fm-3m); Pt; fcc-Ar; hcp-Ar; δ-N2  # identified in Fig. 1 (page 2871)
reference_temperature: 300 K (ambient-T EOS)
sample_heating: CO₂ laser heating to 1800–2474 K with spectroradiometric T measurement
data_quality_notes: |
  Argon PTM produced quasi-hydrostatic conditions. Small nitrogen contamination produced fcc/hcp Ar + δ-N₂ phases.
  Pressure uncertainties from Pt EOS: ΔP ≈ 0.03·P + 4×10⁻⁴·(T–T0).
  Deviatoric stresses estimated ≤ 1 GPa from lattice strain analysis (Singh, 1993 method).
doi: 10.1029/1999JB900364
notes: |
  The ambient-temperature EOS is a third-order Birch–Murnaghan relation with fixed K0 and V0
  and fitted K0′ = 3.94 ± 0.06. This value incorporates data from this study as well as
  Fei (1999), Fiquet et al. (1996), and Utsumi et al. (1998), excluding Mao & Bell (1979).
  High-temperature modeling includes both:
    • T-dependent Birch–Murnaghan EOS
    • Mie–Grüneisen–Debye model with Θ0 ≈ 800 K, γ0 ≈ 1.45, q ≈ 0.8 ± 0.5
  Study provides 61 P–V–T points up to 53 GPa and 2500 K, enabling full thermodynamic parameterization.
  Demonstrates MgO behaves as a Debye-like solid up to ~2500 K and >50 GPa.

Dewaele et al., 2004_Al2O3.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Dewaele et al., 2004
category: 2
composition: Al2O3  # ruby (Cr-doped α-Al2O3 used as pressure calibrant)
method: Luminescence
technique: DAC
DAC_type: membrane DAC with boron-diamond supports
culet: not specified
cal_to_name: Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Mao et al., 1986
cal_to_composition: Cu; Al; Ta; W; Pt; Au; Al2O3 #normialized these and adjusted Mao et al., 1986
eos: Power #PR' = (A/B)[(λ/λ0)^B – 1]
order: #not applicable
A: 1904  # GPa
A_unc: #not specified
B: 9.5
B_unc: #not specified
lambda_0: 694.25  # reference wavelength not given in this study, using value from Shen et al., 2020
max_pressure: 153  # GPa (maximum reached in experiments with metals; ruby calibration extended accordingly)
PTM: He
synchrotron: ESRF, beamline ID30
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: R1 fluorescence line of ruby
reference_temperature: room temperature (298 K)
sample_heating: none
data_quality_notes: |
  The ruby luminescence scale of Mao et al. (1986) was tested 
  against XRD equations of state of six metals (Al, Cu, Ta, W, Pt, Au) 
  measured up to 153 GPa in quasihydrostatic He PTM at ESRF.
  It was concluded that the classical ruby scale underestimates 
  pressure above 100 GPa by up to ~10 GPa. 
  A revised calibration was proposed, keeping the same functional form 
  but with B increased to 9.5. Agreement is within ±3 GPa at 150 GPa.
doi: 10.1103/PhysRevB.70.094112
notes: |
  This calibration is based on static XRD EOS of six metals measured 
  under quasihydrostatic conditions. 
  The revised ruby scale (Eq. 1 in the paper) should be used instead of 
  the classical Mao et al. (1986) scale for pressures above ~30 GPa. 
  Below ~55 GPa, the two scales differ by <2%.

data_quality_notes: |
  Volume uncertainties are ~±0.01 Å³/atom and pressure uncertainties increase from ~0.05 GPa at low pressure to ~2 GPa near 150 GPa (systematic). Ab initio PAW calculations benchmarked against all-electron methods show that GGA-PBE overestimates equilibrium volume, underestimates K₀, and overestimates K₀′, becoming too stiff at high compression, while LDA shows the opposite trend; overall, the Ag dataset is high quality below 100 GPa and well suited for pressure-scale calibration and DFT benchmarking.
doi: [https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevB.78.104102]
notes: |
  Table IV Vinet fit (DAC PR′). V0 fixed from low-P data; K0 and K0′ are fit parameters.

Dewaele et al., 2008_Al2O3.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Dewaele et al., 2008
category: 2
composition: Al2O3  # ruby (Cr-doped α-Al2O3 used as pressure calibrant)
method: Luminescence
technique: DAC
DAC_type: membrane DAC with boron-diamond supports
culet: not specified
cal_to_name: Dewaele et al., 2008; Dewaele et al., 2008; Dewaele et al., 2008; Dewaele et al., 2008
cal_to_composition: Zn; Ni; Mo; Ag #normialized these and adjusted Mao et al., 1986
eos: Power #PR' = (A/B)[(λ/λ0)^B – 1]
order: #not applicable
A: 1920  # GPa
A_unc: #not specified
B: 9.61
B_unc: #not specified
lambda_0: 694.25  # reference wavelength not given in this study, using value from Shen et al., 2020
max_pressure: 156  # GPa (maximum reached in experiments with metals; ruby calibration extended accordingly)
PTM: He
synchrotron: ESRF, beamline ID30
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: R1 fluorescence line of ruby
reference_temperature: room temperature (298 K)
sample_heating: none
data_quality_notes: |
  Data are obtained under near-hydrostatic conditions using helium as a
  pressure medium, with explicit identification and exclusion of data affected by sample
  bridging and nonhydrostatic stress (e.g., Ag and Mo above ~100 GPa). Pressure calibration
  relies on updated ruby fluorescence scales and is cross-validated against ultrasonic and
  reduced shock-wave EoS, reducing systematic uncertainty to ~2–3 GPa at ~150 GPa. Absolute
  pressures at the highest compressions remain model-dependent through the choice of RSW
  datasets and ruby calibration, but internal consistency across metals is high. 
doi: doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevB.78.104102

notes: |
  This calibration is based on static XRD EOS of four metals measured 
  under quasihydrostatic conditions. 
  The revised ruby scale  should be used instead of 
  the classical Mao et al. (1986) scale

    Volume uncertainties are ~±0.01 Å³/atom, and pressure uncertainties increase with compression (systematic ruby-scale component). Ab initio PAW calculations benchmarked against all-electron results show strong internal consistency; however, GGA-PBE slightly overestimates equilibrium volume and overestimates K₀′, leading to a compression curve that becomes too stiff at high pressure, while LDA underestimates V₀ and overestimates K₀. data_quality_notes: |
    Volume uncertainties are ~±0.01 Å³/atom, and pressure uncertainties increase with compression due to systematic ruby-scale effects. Ab initio PAW calculations benchmarked against all-electron methods show strong methodological consistency; however, GGA-PBE slightly overestimates equilibrium volume and K₀′ and becomes too stiff at high compression, while LDA underestimates V₀ and overestimates K₀. Magnetic effects (ferromagnetic ordering in α-Fe) are important for accurate theoretical modeling. Overall, the α-Fe dataset is high quality within the bcc stability field and serves as a key benchmark for pressure calibration and DFT validation.

# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Dewaele et al., 2021
category: 1
composition: Ar
method: XRD
technique: DAC
DAC_type:
culet: 100–300  # µm, depending on run (see Table 1)
cal_to_name: Takemura and Dewaele 2008; Dewaele et al., 2004
cal_to_composition: Au; Al2O3
cal_to_eos: 
cal_to_order:
cal_to_max_pressure:
cal_to_is_K0_fixed:
eos: Rydberg-Vinet #+ Mie-Grüneisen-Debye (MGD, 300 K)
order: 3

V0_per_atom: 38.0   # Å^3/atom (0 K Rydberg–Vinet parameter) 
V0_unc:
K0: 2.65             # GPa (0 K, from low-T measurements)
is_K0_fixed: yes      # fixed to low-temperature literature value
K0_unc:
K0_prime: 7.423
K0_prime_unc:
K_infinity_prime:
K_infinity_prime_unc:

max_pressure: 114     # GPa, highest fcc Ar data at 296 K
PTM: Ar # and Ar–Ne mixtures (59/41%, 40/60%) used to reduce nonhydrostaticity
synchrotron: ESRF ID27; SOLEIL Psiché  # Table 1
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: fcc (111), (200), (220), (311); minor hcp (100), (101) peaks observed

reference_temperature: 296  # K
sample_heating: none (ambient temperature); cryogenic run at 10 K for MGD testing

data_quality_notes: >
  The EOS fit uses quasi-hydrostatic data from Ar crystals embedded in ArNe2 (runs 2–3) 
  combined with pure Ar below 5 GPa. Unit-cell volumes extracted via Rietveld refinement 
  (runs 1–2) and peak fitting (runs 3–5). Non-hydrostatic stress evaluated by gamma-plots 
  from Au lines (Fig. 6). Nonhydrostatic effects are largest below 20 GPa and minimal above 
  ~70 GPa. hcp phase appears at pressures as low as 4.2–13 GPa, but remains <4 vol% up to 114 GPa. 
  EOS parameters validated using low-T isobars in run 5.  # See Figs. 7–10.

doi: 10.1038/s41598-021-93995-y

notes: |
  Argon EOS was determined using angle-dispersive synchrotron XRD on single crystals and 
  powders, in pure Ar and Ar–Ne mixtures. Rydberg–Vinet parameters (V0 = 38.0 Å^3/at, 
  K0 = 2.65 GPa, K0' = 7.423) correspond to the static 0 K part of the equation of state, 
  with thermal pressure added through a quasi-harmonic Mie–Grüneisen–Debye model:
      P(V,T) = P0(V) + Pth(V,T).
  The thermal model uses:
      γ(V) = 2.20·(V/V0) + 0.5        # eq. (4)
      θD(V) = 93.3 * (1/x) * exp[2.20(1 - x)],  x = V/V0  # eq. (5)
  These parameters reproduce thermal expansion and P–V data from 1–114 GPa at 296 K and 
  10–296 K in cryogenic runs. hcp-Ar appears through stacking faults but remains a minor 
  phase (<4%). fcc → hcp transition is stress-assisted and not thermodynamically complete 
  up to at least 114 GPa.

  This EOS can be used as a metrological pressure standard for experiments using Ar as a 
  pressure medium at ambient or elevated temperature, as the quasi-harmonic model captures 
  thermal pressure reliably up to high temperatures.

# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Ding et al., 2025
category: 1
composition: W
method: XRD
technique: tDAC
DAC_type: #modified toroidal (mt-DAC), Boehler–Almax force pattern
culet: 16–17  # µm (Table I)
cal_to_name: Dewaele et al., 2018; Akahama and Kawamura 2010
cal_to_composition: Au; Diamond
cal_to_eos: Rydberg-Vinet; Akahama10_Equation
cal_to_order:
cal_to_max_pressure:
cal_to_is_K0_fixed:
eos: Rydberg-Vinet
order: 3
V0: 31.716  # Å^3 (fixed)  # from RV fit description, page 4
V0_unc: 
K0: 298.6     # GPa (fixed in their fit)
is_K0_fixed: yes
K0_unc:
K0_prime: 4.103
K0_prime_unc: 0.029
K_infinity_prime:
K_infinity_prime_unc:

max_pressure: 527  # GPa
PTM: None #(W is both sample and gasket); ultrahigh-pressure nonhydrostatic environment
synchrotron: ESRF ID27 (run 1), SSRF BL15U1 (runs 2–8)
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: bcc W (110), (200), (211), (220), (310); Au peaks for pressure
reference_temperature: 296  # K
sample_heating: none
data_quality_notes: >
  W retains the bcc structure up to 527 GPa (Fig. 2). Pressures were determined using the 
  Au t-DAC pressure scale of Dewaele et al. (2018), validated against Fratanduono et al. 
  (shockless Au) and diamond Raman in some comparative runs. XRD profiles above 300 GPa 
  show peak broadening due to nonhydrostatic stresses typical of ultrahigh-pressure t-DAC 
  experiments. Uniaxial stresses estimated by peak-width method (Supplemental Fig. S4). 
  EOS comparison with Dewaele (2004) and Dubrovinsky (2012) indicates consistency to 250 GPa.
doi: 10.1103/PhysRevB.111.214101
notes: |
  The W P–V data were fitted using the Rydberg–Vinet (RV) equation of state:
     P(V) = 3K0 * (1 - x) / x^2 * exp[ (3/2)(K0' - 1)(1 - x) ]
     where x = (V/V0)^(1/3).
  For their global ultrahigh-pressure RV fit:
     V0 was fixed to 31.716 Å^3,
     K0 fixed to 298.6 GPa,
     K0' = 4.103 ± 0.029.
  Data extend to 527(7) GPa in run 1 using a Boehler–Almax mt-DAC. W remains bcc throughout 
  compression. Authors note excellent agreement with quasihydrostatic W EOS below 200 GPa 
  (Dewaele 2004; Dewaele 2019). High-pressure deviations relative to shock Hugoniot EOS 
  expected due to temperature and strength effects.
  This EOS should be classified as a static, ultrahigh-pressure RV scale dependent on an Au 
  reference scale measured in the same toroidal DAC configuration.

Dorogokupets and Dewaele 2007_Au.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Dorogokupets and Dewaele 2007
category: 2
composition: Au
method: XRD
technique: Expanded Mie–Grüneisen–Debye (EMGD) model
DAC_type: #not specified
culet: #not specified
cal_to_name: Takemura 2001; Dewaele et al., 2004; Chijioke et al., 2005
cal_to_composition: Au; Au; Au
eos: Rydberg-Vinet
order: 3
V0_cc_mole: 10.215 #assumed same as Dorogokupets and Oganov 2005, not explicitly stated in text 
K0: 167 # GPa
is_K0_fixed:
K0_unc:
K0_prime: 5.9
K0_prime_unc:
max_pressure: 200   # GPa, approximate extrapolation, not stated in text
PTM: not applicable (model-based compilation)
reference_temperature: 298 K
sample_heating: none
data_quality_notes: |
  This EOS is not a simple experimental fit. It is derived from a global thermodynamic
  model (EMGD) constrained by shock-wave, XRD, thermochemical, and elastic data.
  The cold curve is approximated using a Vinet-type formulation, but full pressure
  requires thermal contributions (Grüneisen, anharmonic, electronic).
  Reported parameters represent an internally consistent pressure scale.

doi: 10.1080/08957950701659700

notes: |
  - The Au EOS is constructed as part of a multi-calibrant internally consistent framework.
  - K0 = 167 GPa and K0' ≈ 5.9 represent the preferred average of recent quasihydrostatic data.
  - The formulation includes intrinsic anharmonicity and electronic contributions,
    which are required for accurate high-temperature behavior.
  - Differences of up to ~4 GPa can occur relative to other scales at high P–T.
  - Not directly equivalent to a standalone BM3 or Vinet fit used in static DAC studies.

study: Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007
category: 1

sample_heating: none
data_quality_notes:
doi: 10.1103/PhysRevB.75.024115
notes: |
  Abstract: "We have constructed semiempirical equations of state of Al, Au, Cu, Pt, Ta, and W, which within experimental
  error bars describe the available shock-wave, ultrasonic, x-ray, and thermochemical data in the temperature
  range from 10–20 K up to the melting temperature and to compression x=V/V0=0.5–0.7. The
  comparison of the calculated room-temperature isotherms for these metals with quasihydrostatic measurements
  supports recently proposed ruby pressure scales. We recommend a new ruby pressure scale in the form ...Polynomial... with parameters A=1884 GPa and m=5.5. The cross check on independent data
  confirms the obtained PVT equations of state of Ag, Al, Au, Cu, Pt, Ta, W, MgO, and diamond. The equations
  of state of these materials obtained here provide accurate and versatile means for calibrating pressure at all
  temperatures below the melting point. Furthermore, they can be used for accurate tabulation of thermodynamic
  properties (heat capacities, entropies) of these reference substances in a wide P-T range.

cal_to_name: Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Occelli et al., 2003; Zha et al., 2000; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007; Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007

data_quality_notes: |
  Dorogokupets and Oganov (2007) constructed semiempirical EOSs for several
  metals and MgO from shock-wave, ultrasonic, x-ray, and thermochemical data.
  These were used to cross-check and refine ruby pressure scales. The final 
  recommended ruby scale was expressed in the form:
    P = 1884 * (Δλ/λ0) * [1 + 5.5*(Δλ/λ0)].
  This scale agrees with recalibrated metal EOS up to ~160 GPa and provides a 
  single consistent description up to ~300 GPa. Deviations are typically ≤1–2 GPa
  compared to independent isotherm measurements.
doi: 10.1103/PhysRevB.75.024115
notes: |
  Recommended ruby scale: P = 1884(Δλ/λ0)(1 + 5.5 Δλ/λ0), valid up to ~300 GPa. 
  Authors emphasize improved internal consistency with Al, Au, Cu, Pt, Ta, W, MgO,
  and diamond EOS compared to earlier calibrations.

  comparison of the calculated room-temperature isotherms for these metals with quasihydrostatic measurements
  supports recently proposed ruby pressure scales. We recommend a new ruby pressure scale in the form ...Polynomial... with parameters A=1884 GPa and m=5.5. The cross check on independent data
  confirms the obtained PVT equations of state of Ag, Al, Au, Cu, Pt, Ta, W, MgO, and diamond. The equations
  of state of these materials obtained here provide accurate and versatile means for calibrating pressure at all
  temperatures below the melting point. Furthermore, they can be used for accurate tabulation of thermodynamic
  properties (heat capacities, entropies) of these reference substances in a wide P-T range.

Dubrovinsky et al., 2012_Re_BM4.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Dubrovinsky et al., 2012
category: 1
composition: Re
method: XRD
technique: DAC
DAC_type: Double-stage DAC with NCD semi-ball secondary anvils + conventional DAC
culet: 250–300   # μm primary anvils; 12–20 μm NCD semi-balls as second-stage anvils
cal_to_name: Yokoo et al., 2009
cal_to_composition: Au
eos: Birch-Murnaghan
order: 4
V0_cc_mole: 8.87 
V0_unc: 0.01
K0: 342                # GPa (K300 = 342(6) GPa)
is_K0_fixed: no
K0_unc: 6
K0_prime: 6.15
K0_prime_unc: 0.15
K_infinity_prime:
K_infinity_prime_unc:
K0_dprime: -0.029    # GPa^-1
K0_dprime_unc: 0.004
max_pressure: 640          # GPa
PTM: Paraffin; Ne; He
synchrotron: 13-IDD APS
peaks_used_to_fit_sample:
reference_temperature: 300 K
sample_heating: none
data_quality_notes: |
    Fourth-order Birch–Murnaghan (BM4) EOS fitted to the combined data set:
      • Experiment no. 1: Re + Au mixture between NCD semi-ball secondary anvils,
        paraffin pressure medium; pressures 416–640 GPa from Au EOS.
      • Experiment no. 2: Pure Re between NCD semi-balls, Ne pressure medium;
        pressures 441–608 GPa determined from the Re EOS of Experiment no. 1.
      • Experiment no. 3: Re powder in He in a conventional DAC, 0–165 GPa.
    The global BM4 fit yields:
      K300 = 342(6) GPa
      K0' = 6.15(15)
      K0'' = -0.029(4) GPa^-1
      V0 = 8.87(1) cm^3/mol = 29.46(1) Å^3 per unit cell
    The authors show that BM3 and Vinet EOS fitted only to ≤165 GPa data
    cannot reproduce the 400–640 GPa measurements, while this BM4 EOS does.
doi: 10.1038/ncomms2160
notes: |
    This is the recommended ultra-high-pressure EOS for hcp-Re from Dubrovinsky
    et al. (2012), valid from ambient pressure to ~640 GPa.
    It combines conventional DAC and double-stage DAC data and explicitly
    includes the second pressure derivative of the bulk modulus, K0''.
    This BM4 EOS resolves discrepancies among previous Re EOS determinations
    and demonstrates that static data in the ~0.5–0.6 TPa range are essential
    for such a low-compressibility material.
Duffy et al., 1995 MgO.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Duffy et al., 1995
category: 2
composition: MgO  # B1 (NaCl-type) structure
method: XRD
technique: DAC
DAC_type: Mao-Bell DAC with beveled diamonds
culet: 19.5; 49.8  # um (central flats)
cal_to_name: Carter et al., 1971
cal_to_composition: Mo
eos: Birch-Murnaghan
order: 3
V0_cc_mole: 11.253  # cm^3/mol; inferred from previous sentence referencing an unpublished study from Fei. 
V0_unc:
P0: 0
K0: 177  # GPa
is_K0_fixed: no
K0_unc: 4
K0_prime: 4.0
K0_prime_unc: 0.1
max_pressure: 227  # GPa
PTM: None
synchrotron: NSLS (superconducting wiggler radiation)
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: 200; 220; 420
reference_temperature: 300 K
sample_heating: none
data_quality_notes: |
  Energy-dispersive synchrotron XRD measurements of MgO were
  conducted to ultrahigh pressures without a pressure-transmitting
  medium. Strong nonhydrostatic stresses were present and explicitly
  quantified via lattice-strain analysis using differences between
  the (200) and (220) reflections.

  Pressure was determined from molybdenum diffraction lines using
  an isotherm derived from shock compression data. MgO remained
  in the B1 (NaCl) structure to the maximum pressure investigated.
doi: 10.1103/PhysRevLett.74.1371
notes: |
  A third-order Birch–Murnaghan EOS fit to the present static
  compression data yields K0 = 177(4) GPa and K0' = 4.0(1),
  which is significantly stiffer than ultrasonic and quasihydrostatic
  results due to nonhydrostatic stress.

  The authors demonstrate that when shear strength (≥11 GPa at
  227 GPa) is taken into account, a single EOS can reconcile
  static, ultrasonic, and shock data. However, the parameters
  reported here correspond strictly to the static DAC dataset
  without stress correction.

  This EOS should therefore NOT be treated as a hydrostatic
  reference; it is most appropriate for studies explicitly
  considering strength effects at multimegabar pressures.

reference_temperature: 300 K

data_quality_notes: |
  XRD and Au calibrant measured simultaneously at the same spot to minimize pressure gradients.
  He pressure medium produced highly hydrostatic conditions, improving EOS accuracy vs earlier studies.
  EOS accuracy dominated by c-axis uncertainties (0.1%).

doi: 10.1103/PhysRevB.72.094113

notes: |
  The EOS is noticeably stiffer than earlier non-hydrostatic studies and agrees with dynamic
  shock-derived curves. No phase transitions observed to 182 GPa. The increasing c/a ratio
  toward 1.60 at 180 GPa suggests more isotropic bonding at high P.

reference_temperature: 300 K
sample_heating: none
data_quality_notes: |
  Pure Pd metal was compressed in neon and measured by powder XRD
  up to ~31 GPa to establish reference compressibility data for
  comparison with Pd carbides and hydrides synthesized at HP–HT.
  No structural phase transitions were observed over the pressure
  range investigated.
doi: 10.1016/j.jallcom.2020.156179
notes: |
  EOS parameters are derived from a third-order Birch–Murnaghan fit
  to Pd P–V data collected during compression in Ne (DAC1, Table 1;
  Fig. 1). The fitted parameters are:
  V0 = 58.868(2) Å^3, K0 = 157(3) GPa, K0' = 9.9(4).

  The authors note that the unusually high K0' value is atypical for
  transition metals and may reflect the limited pressure range
  (≤31 GPa). When K0' is fixed to the value reported by Mao et al.
  (1978), a higher K0 (~190 GPa) is obtained.

  This Pd EOS is intended as an internal reference within the study
  and should not be treated as a high-pressure primary calibration.

Fei et al., 2016_Fe.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Fei et al., 2016
category: 2
composition: Fe-hcp
method: XRD
technique: DAC
DAC_type: DAC
culet: 100 #um
cal_to_name: Fei et al., 2007; Fei et al., 2007; Fei et al., 2007; Tange et al., 2009
cal_to_composition: Ne; NaCl-B2; Pt; MgO
cal_to_eos: 
eos: Birch-Murnaghan
order: 3
V0: 22.4277        # Å^3 (not explicitly reported; ρ0 is fixed at 8.2695 g/cm3, calculated v0)
V0_unc:
K0: 172.7           # GPa
is_K0_fixed: no     # ρ0 was fixed, K0 was fitted, manuscript also reports no yaml file made for these values (K0 = 191.44(±5.3)GPa, K0′= 4.52(±0.08), and ρ0 = 8.3602(±0.0241) g/cm3).
K0_unc: 1.4
K0_prime: 4.79
K0_prime_unc: 0.05

max_pressure: 280   # GPa (global 300 K fit range including previous data) 
PTM: Ne; MgO; NaCl  # Ne quasi-hydrostatic to 100 GPa; MgO medium to 205 GPa
gasket: Re
synchrotron: APS GSECARS 13-ID-D (Advanced Photon Source, Argonne)
peaks_used_to_fit_sample:
reference_temperature: 300  # K
sample_heating: room-temperature compression for EOS; double-sided laser heating in Ne medium up to 1800 K for thermal EOS (Fe and Pt heated separately) 
data_quality_notes: >
  New hcp-Fe compression data at 300 K were collected in Ne (up to 100 GPa) and MgO (up to 205 GPa)
  pressure media, using Ne, NaCl-B2, Pt, and MgO as pressure markers on internally consistent scales
  (Fei et al., 2007; Tange et al., 2009). When pressures for previous studies (Mao et al., 1990; Dubrovinsky
  et al., 2000; Yamazaki et al., 2012; Sakai et al., 2014) are recalculated onto these intercalibrated scales,
  all room-temperature hcp-Fe compression data agree within ~2% in pressure up to 280 GPa (Figures 1–2). Publised data was fit with this study
doi: 10.1002/2016GL069456
notes: |
  Room-temperature EOS:
    • All 300 K hcp-Fe data (this study + Dewaele et al. 2006 + recalibrated Mao 1990, Dubrovinsky 2000,
      Yamazaki 2012, Sakai 2014) were fitted to a 3rd-order Birch–Murnaghan equation of state:
          P_300K = (3/2) K0 [ (ρ/ρ0)^(7/3) - (ρ/ρ0)^(5/3) ] *
                    { 1 + (3/4)(K0' - 4)[ (ρ/ρ0)^(2/3) - 1 ] }.
      With fixed initial density ρ0 = 8.2695 g/cm^3, the preferred fit yields:
          K0 = 172.7 ± 1.4 GPa, K0' = 4.79 ± 0.05. 
    • If ρ0 is left free, the alternative fit gives K0 = 191.44 ± 5.3 GPa, K0' = 4.52 ± 0.08, ρ0 = 8.3602 ± 0.0241 g/cm^3,
      but Fei et al. (2016) favor the fixed-ρ0 solution to remain consistent with prior density measurements and
      to minimize tradeoff between K0 and ρ0.
    • Residuals of all included data sets relative to the preferred BM3 fit are generally within ±2% in pressure
      up to ~280 GPa (Figure 2).
  Experimental setup:
    • Ne PTM runs (c112, c116, c118) used 100 µm beveled culets, Re gaskets preindented to 18 µm with 60 µm
      sample chambers; Ne, NaCl-B2, and Pt were co-loaded as pressure markers. 
    • Higher-pressure MgO runs (c114, c117) used FIB-milled stepped anvils with 20 µm top faces and MgO
      discs plus cBN inserts, achieving pressures to 205 GPa.
    • XRD data were collected with a MAR CCD detector at λ = 0.3344 Å; unit-cell parameters for Fe and
      pressure standards were obtained via Fit2D processing and indexing. 
  Thermal EOS:
    • Thermal pressures P_th = P - P_300K were derived from:
        – Static P–V–T data for Fe in Ne + Pt up to 100 GPa and 1800 K, and
        – Shock Hugoniot data for Fe up to ~200 GPa and 5000 K (Brown et al., 2000), referenced to the 300 K
          BM3 isotherm of this study.
    • The thermal pressure model combines quasi-harmonic Debye vibrational contributions with an empirical
      anharmonic + electronic term:
          P - P_300K = (γ_vib/V) ∫_300^T C_V,vib dT + (γ_e/V) ∫_300^T C_V,e dT,
      where γ_vib = γ0 (ρ0/ρ)^q with γ0 = 1.74, q = 0.78; C_V,e = β0 (ρ0/ρ)^k T with β0 = 0.07 J kg^-1 K^-2,
      k = 1.34; θ0 = 422 K; γ_e = 2. 
    • The resulting thermal EOS reproduces shock, static high-T XRD, and phonon DOS–derived thermal pressures
      for hcp-Fe and is used by Fei et al. (2016) to compute inner-core density and density deficit (~3.6% at
      the ICB for T_ICB = 6000 K).

# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Fiquet et al., 1996
category: 1

DAC_type: membrane DAC with laser heating

cal_to_composition: Al2O3
eos: Birch-Murnaghan
order: 3
V0: 74.698 # Å^3
V0_unc:
K0: 164  # GPa
is_K0_fixed: no
K0_unc: 2
K0_prime: 4.5
max_pressure: 16  # GPa
PTM: Ar
synchrotron: LURE DCI DW11
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: 111; 200; 220
reference_temperature: 300 K
sample_heating: CO2 laser (used for stress relaxation only) this yaml only reports unheated EOS, if heated for stress relation change K0 to 177+-2 GPa
data_quality_notes: |
  Room-temperature compression data for MgO were collected
  before and after laser-heating cycles. Laser heating was
  used to reduce deviatoric stress; EOS parameters reported
  here are derived from room-temperature data only.

  Pressures were determined primarily from the equation of
  state of solid argon, which the authors argue better
  represents the pressure at the sample position than ruby
  fluorescence measured at the gasket edge.
doi: 10.1016/0031-9201(95)03109-X
notes: |
  A third-order Birch–Murnaghan EOS was fit to the 300 K
  compression data. When K0' is fixed at 4.5 (from Mao &
  Bell, 1979), the authors obtain K0 = 164(2) GPa using
  argon pressures, in good agreement with ultrasonic
  measurements (Jackson & Niesler, 1982; Isaak et al., 1989).

  Free fits yield unrealistically low K0' (~1.5), which the
  authors attribute to the limited pressure range (≤16 GPa).

  This EOS should be treated as a quasi-hydrostatic,
  low-pressure MgO reference suitable for comparison with
  ultrasonic and moderate-pressure DAC datasets.

  increase lattice volume and bias fitted compressibility.

# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Goncharov et al., 2005
category: 1
composition: cBN
method: Raman
technique: DAC (Raman spectroscopy; resistive + laser heating)
DAC_type: symmetric DAC
culet: not specified
cal_to_name: Not Specified
cal_to_composition: Al2O3
eos: Raman_Goncharov
# High-T Raman pressure scale based on TO phonon (Eq. 2)
#   P = (K / b) * [ (ω / ω0)^b - 1 ]
# where K, ω0, and b are temperature dependent

# Temperature-dependent coefficients (explicitly given in text)
A: 322.5764    #324.5-6.13*10^-3*T-0.94*10^-6*T^2; T=300
B: 3.43463    #3.47-8.61*10^-5*T-1.06*10^-7*T^2;  T=300
V0: 1055.94    #1060.2-0.010*T-1.40*10^-5*T^2; T=300

max_pressure: 40     # GPa
PTM: Ar; Al2O3
synchrotron: none
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: c-BN TO Raman mode (~1055–1060 cm⁻¹)
reference_temperature: 300–2300   # K
sample_heating: internal resistive heating + laser heating
data_quality_notes: |
  Raman TO-mode frequencies measured to 40 GPa and 1750 K (laser heating),
  and to 2300 K at 26 GPa (internal heating). Pressure at 300 K was measured
  using ruby fluorescence and assumed constant upon heating; thermal
  pressure was estimated to be ≤1.5 GPa.

  The pressure scale is constructed by separating quasiharmonic (volume-
  dependent) and intrinsic anharmonic contributions to the TO-mode shift.
  This is the first experimentally constrained pressure scale valid above
  1000 K for c-BN.

doi: 10.1103/PhysRevB.72.100104
notes: |
  Pressure scale given by Eq. (2):
    P = (K(T)/b(T)) * [ (ω/ω0(T))^b(T) - 1 ].

  Temperature-dependent parameters:
    K(T) = 324.5 - 6.13×10⁻3 T - 0.94×10⁻6 T²  (GPa)
    ω0(T) = 1060.2 - 0.010 T - 1.40×10⁻5 T²  (cm⁻¹)
    b(T) = 3.47 - 8.61×10⁻5 T - 1.06×10⁻7 T²

  This Raman scale is intended for high-temperature use and should not
  be extrapolated beyond ~40 GPa without independent EOS constraints.

  Nitrogen (to 35 GPa) and sodium (to 69 GPa) were used as quasihydrostatic media.
  Diffraction peak widths in Na remained at the instrumental limit, demonstrating
  extremely hydrostatic conditions. Ta peak widths were slightly larger due to
  built-in microstrain in the commercial Ta powder.

  The P–V data were fit with the Holzapfel (H02) EOS (n = 5), yielding
  B0 = 207.6 ± 1.5 GPa and B0' = 2.85 ± 0.10. The fitted ambient a0 = 3.3135 Å
  agrees with the independently measured a0 = 3.3139(3) Å.

  The authors showed that the H02 fit describes both their data (0–69 GPa)
  and the high-pressure Au-calibrated data of Cynn & Yoo (70–174 GPa)
  within uncertainties, providing a single consistent EOS for Ta across
  the entire bcc stability range (to 174 GPa).
doi: 10.1063/1.1454200
notes: >
  The H02 formulation was chosen over Birch-Murnaghan because BM3 gives unphysical
  curvature at small volumes (B' < 4). The parameters reported here reproduce Ta’s
  compression smoothly across all known experimental datasets and are commonly used
  when a ruby-calibrated EOS is required. The value V0 = 18.191 Å^3/atom corresponds
  to a0 = 3.3135 Å for bcc Ta.

data_quality_notes: |
    Neon crystallizes in the fcc structure at 4.7 GPa and remains fcc to at least 110 GPa (Fig. 1). Unit-cell parameters and molar volumes were determined from energy-dispersive synchrotron XRD. Pressures were determined from ruby fluorescence (primary) and W XRD (secondary) for cross-checking. 
    The temperature reduction to 0 K uses a Mie-Grüneisen–Debye model with Debye temperature θ and zero-point + thermal pressure terms P_zp(V) and P_th(V). The 300-K isotherm agrees closely with electronic-structure calculations and is significantly softer than pure pair-potential models.
doi: 10.1103/PhysRevB.39.11820
notes: |
    This paper reports high-pressure X-ray diffraction measurements of solid neon up to 110 GPa at 300 K, showing that neon remains an insulating face-centered cubic (fcc) solid throughout this range while undergoing large compression (down to ~28% of its original volume) . The experimentally determined pressure–volume equation of state agrees well with electronic structure calculations but is not accurately described by traditional two-body (pair) interatomic potentials, which overestimate stiffness at high pressures. Additionally, the study validates and extends the ruby fluorescence pressure scale beyond 100 GPa, contributing to improved high-pressure measurement techniques.

# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Holmes et al., 1989
category: 1
composition: Pt  # fcc platinum
method: XRD #Shock (two-stage light-gas gun) + first-principles theory
technique: Shock compression (impedance matching to Ta)
cal_to_name: Absolute #technically related toMitchell and Nellis, 1981 (Ta Hugoniot impedance-match standard); McQueen et al., 1970 (Ta/Pt shock database)
cal_to_composition: Pt
eos: Rydberg-Vinet
order: 3
V0: 60.38 # Å^3/unit cell (experimental equilibrium atomic volume; converted from 101.9 a.u.^3)
V0_unc:
K0: 266  # GPa (BT at 300 K, from Vinet-fit to theoretical 300 K isotherm)
is_K0_fixed: no
K0_unc:
K0_prime: 5.81
K0_prime_unc:
max_pressure: 660  # GPa (shock Hugoniot measurements); 300 K isotherm analytic fit stated valid to ~550 GPa
PTM: not applicable
reference_temperature: 300
data_quality_notes: |
  Pt was shock-compressed (Ta flyer / Ta standard) and the Pt Hugoniot was
  characterized by a linear Us–up relation over 32–660 GPa. A first-principles
  (LMTO) cold curve plus thermal terms was used to compute the Hugoniot and
  a 300 K isotherm; agreement between calculated and measured Hugoniot
  qualifies the 300 K isotherm to ~10% in pressure at a given density.

  The paper provides an analytic “universal EOS” representation of the
  *theoretical* 300 K isotherm in Vinet form.
doi: 10.1063/1.344177
notes: |
  Universal EOS (Vinet form) used for the 300 K isotherm:
    P300(X) = PT * [(1 - X)/X^2] * exp[η(1 - X)],
    where X = (V/V0)^(1/3), PT = 3*BT, and η = 1.5*(B' - 1).

  Holmes et al. (1989) report the equilibrium volume V0 in atomic units
  (a.u.^3 per atom), where 1 a.u. is the Bohr radius (a0 = 0.529177 Å).
  Conversion to crystallographic units was performed as:

    1 a.u.^3 = (0.529177 Å)^3 = 0.1481847 Å^3

  The reported value V0 = 101.9 a.u.^3/atom therefore corresponds to:

    V0 = 15.095 Å^3/atom

  Platinum crystallizes in the fcc structure with Z = 4 atoms per
  conventional unit cell, so the unit-cell volume used here is:

    V0 = 60.38 Å^3/unit cell

  This value is consistent with ambient-pressure lattice parameters
  of fcc Pt (a ≈ 3.92 Å) and is used as the reference volume for the
  Vinet (universal) 300 K isotherm derived in this study.
Holzapfel 2001_Ag.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Holzapfel et al., 2001
category: 1
composition: Ag
method: XRD
technique: DAC #Analysis (shock + ultrasonic constraints; thermodynamic modeling)
DAC_type: not specified
culet: not specified
cal_to_name: Not Specified #References 54, 36-45, and 43-45 in paper
cal_to_composition: Ag
eos: AP2
equation_full: |
  # AP2 Equation of State (Holzapfel AP2 form)
  #
  # p(V) = 3*K0 * (1 - x)/x^5 * [1 + c2*x*(1 - x)] * exp[c0*(1 - x)]
  #
  # where:
  #   x = (V / V0)^(1/3)
  #
  # AP2 parameters:
  #   c0 = - ln(3*K0 / a_FG) - (5/3) * ln(V0 / Z)
  #   c2 = (3/2)*(K0' - 3) - c0
  #
  # Constants:
  #   a_FG = 0.02337 GPa·nm^5   (Fermi-gas constant)

V0_per_atom: 17.057       # Å^3/atom  (Table 1: V0R = 0.017057 nm^3)
V0_unc: 0.001             # Å^3/atom  (Table 1: ±0.000001 nm^3)

K0: 101.0                 # GPa (Table 1, TR = 300 K)
K0_unc: 0.2               # GPa
is_K0_fixed: no

K0_prime: 6.15            # (Table 1, TR = 300 K)
K0_prime_unc: 0.15

atomic_number: 47
max_pressure: 500         # GPa (paper scope: up to 500 GPa and above)
PTM: Not Applicable
synchrotron: Not Applicable
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: Not Applicable
reference_temperature: 300 K
sample_heating: none
atomic_number: 47

data_quality_notes: |
  Parameters V0, K0, and K0' at TR = 300 K are the primary input values
  listed in Table 1 for Ag (based on literature constraints as compiled in this paper).
  The AP2 form (Eq. 13) is used for representing isotherms in the framework developed here.

doi: 10.1063/1.1370170
notes: |
    This paper develops a comprehensive and thermodynamically consistent set of equations of state (EOS) for Cu, Ag, and Au over wide pressure (up to ≥500 GPa) and temperature ranges by combining shock-wave data, ultrasonic measurements, and theoretical modeling. Using a refined Mie–Grüneisen framework with a modified pseudo-Debye–Einstein model for thermal effects, the authors resolve longstanding discrepancies between dynamic (shock) and static data by imposing new constraints on the volume dependence of the Grüneisen parameter. They show that commonly used EOS forms (e.g., Birch or Vinet) can lead to inaccuracies at high compression, while their preferred AP2 formulation provides a more reliable representation consistent with both experimental datasets.

Holzapfel 2003_Al203.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Holzapfel 2003
category: 2
composition: Al2O3  # ruby (Cr-doped α-Al2O3)
method: Luminescence #(R1 fluorescence)
technique: DAC
DAC_type: not specified
culet: not specified
cal_to_name: McSkimin and Andreatcg 1972; Lowrie and Gonas 1964; Simmons and Wang 1971; Sarrao et al. 1994; Zha et al., 2000  
cal_to_composition: Diamond; W; W; Ta; MgO
eos: HP02Ruby
equation_full: |
  #Modified HP02 functional form (Eq. 3 in the paper), given both ways:
#  λ(p) form:
#    λ = λ0 * [ 1 - (C / (B + C)) * ln( 1 + ((B + C)/A) * p ) ]^(-1/C)
#  p(λ) form:
 #   p = (A / (B + C)) * { exp[ ((B + C)/C) * ( 1 - (λ/λ0)^(-C) ) ] - 1 }
A: 1820        # GPa (best estimate)
A_unc: 30
B: 14          # initial curvature parameter (best estimate)
B_unc: 2
C: 7.3         # additional curvature parameter (best fit; see C_range)
lambda_0: 694.24 # not specified, assumed value based on ambient R1 wavelength symbolized as λ0 in the paper
max_pressure: 150  # GPa (range over which comparisons/constraints are discussed)
PTM: Not Applicable # (this paper is an analysis); source datasets used He (diamond), Ne (W), Ar in earlier MX86 work
synchrotron: not specified
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: R1 line of ruby fluorescence
reference_temperature: 300 K
sample_heating: none
data_quality_notes: |
  The study refines the ruby pressure scale by comparing "nearly hydrostatic"
  DAC XRD data on diamond (He PTM) and Ta with ultrasonic and shock-wave EOS results,
  and by honoring the absolute MgO-based calibration to 55 GPa.
  The revised scale lowers A relative to earlier values and significantly increases curvature (B),
  while introducing an additional parameter C to capture high-pressure behavior.
  Best estimates - A = 1820(30) GPa, B = 14(2), C = 7.3 (reasonable range C = 5–10).
doi: 10.1063/1.1525856
notes: Propose 3 sets of parameters. Another based on Ta and dimaond A=1790, B=17 C=9.5. Thee other for diamond with Kp of 3.4 --> A=1850, B=12, C=6. This Yaml file assume the diamond data with Kp of 3.7. The authers viewed this as the best fit to the data

Holzapfel 2005_Al203.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Holzapfel 2005
category: 1
composition: Al2O3  # ruby (Cr-doped α-Al2O3)
method: Luminescence
technique: DAC
DAC_type: not specified
culet: not specified
cal_to_name: Occelli et al., 2003; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004;
cal_to_composition: Diamond; Cu; Au; W; Al; Pt; Ta
eos: HP02Ruby #Modified-Freund–Ingalls
equation_full: |
  # Modified Freund–Ingalls / HP02 functional form (Eq. 3 in Holzapfel 2005):
  # λ(p) form:
  #   λ = λ0 * [ 1 - (C / (B + C)) * ln( 1 + ((B + C)/A) * p ) ]^(-1/C)
  #
  # p(λ) form:
  #   p = (A / (B + C)) * { exp[ ((B + C)/C) * ( 1 - (λ0/λ)^C ) ] - 1 }
  #
  # where:
  #   p  = pressure (GPa)
  #   λ0 = ambient-pressure R1 wavelength
  #   λ  = R1 wavelength at pressure p
A: 1845        # GPa
A_unc:
B: 14.7        # dimensionless
B_unc:
C: 7.5         # dimensionless
C_unc:
lambda_0: 694.2   # nm (ruby R1 ambient wavelength used in Holzapfel, 2005)
max_pressure: Not Applicable
PTM: Not Applicable # (this is an analysis paper); underlying datasets use He (diamond, Cu, Au, W, Al, Pt, Ta) and earlier Ar/Ne calibrations
synchrotron: not specified  # original XRD data from Occelli et al. 2003; Dewaele et al. 2004
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: R1 line of ruby fluorescence
reference_temperature: 300 K
sample_heating: none
data_quality_notes: |
  The study refines a practical ruby pressure scale by simultaneously fitting high-pressure
  single-crystal XRD data on diamond, Cu, Au, W, Al, Pt, and Ta (mostly in He as PTM)
  together with ruby R1 luminescence shifts, using AP2 EOS forms for the calibrants.
  V0 and K0 are fixed to literature/ultrasonic values, and K0' for each calibrant plus the
  ruby parameters A, B, and C are co-fitted.
  The modified Freund–Ingalls (HP02-type) form provides a better representation of the
  ruby line shift than the older modified Murnaghan form, especially at higher pressures.
  The recommended parameters are A = 1845 GPa, B = 14.7, C = 7.5, with an estimated
  uncertainty of ±25 GPa in A; corresponding bounds imply B ≈ 13.0–16.3 and C ≈ 5.5–9.3.
doi: 10.1080/09511920500147501
notes: >
  This YAML captures the 2005 refit of the HP02/modified Freund–Ingalls ruby scale using
  AP2 EOS forms for Cu, Au, diamond, W, Al, Pt, and Ta. The preferred parameters
  (A = 1845 GPa, B = 14.7, C = 7.5) are obtained from a correlated fit with V0 and K0
  fixed to literature values and K0' and ruby A, B, C treated as free parameters.
  Holzapfel also reports alternative ruby parameter sets based on different assumptions
  and datasets (e.g., polynomial fits and other EOS forms), but this file adopts the AP2-based
  global fit that the author identifies as the most consistent realization of a practical
  pressure scale in the 1–300 GPa range.

composition: Ta
method: XRD
technique: DAC
DAC_type: not specified
culet: not specified
cal_to_name: Holzapfel 2005; Villars et al., 1997; Occelli et al., 2003; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004; Dewaele et al., 2004;
cal_to_composition: Al2O3; Ta; Diamond; Cu; Au; W; Al; Pt; Ta
eos: AP2
equation_full: |
  # AP2 Equation of State (Holzapfel AP2 form)
  #
  # p(V) = 3*K0 * (1 - x)/x^5 * [1 + c2*x*(1 - x)] * exp[c0*(1 - x)]
  #
  # where:
  #   x = (V / V0)^(1/3)
  #
  # AP2 parameters:
  #   c0 = - ln(3*K0 / a_FG) - (5/3) * ln(V0 / Z)
  #   c2 = (3/2)*(K0' - 3) - c0
  #
  # Constants:
  #   a_FG = 0.02337 GPa·nm^5   (Fermi-gas constant)

V0_per_atom: 18.014  # Å^3 (converted from 0.011789 nm^3, Table 1 Vo_fit)
V0_unc:
K0: 194  # GPa, fixed 
K0_unc:
is_K0_fixed: no
K0_prime: 3.82
K0_prime_unc:

atomic_number: 73
max_pressure: Not Applicable
PTM: Not Applicable # (this is an analysis paper); underlying datasets use He (diamond, Cu, Au, W, Al, Pt, Ta) and earlier Ar/Ne calibrations
synchrotron:
peaks_used_to_fit_sample:
reference_temperature: 300 K
sample_heating: none
data_quality_notes: |
  The EOS parameters are the AP2-fit values (Vo_fit, Ko_fit, K0'fit) taken from the
  upper section of Table 1 in Holzapfel (2005), evaluated after adopting the HP05 ruby
  scale with parameters A = 1845 GPa, B = 14.7, and C = 7.5. These fit values represent
  the EOS derived purely from the single-crystal XRD data recalculated using the updated
  ruby scale. Literature (Ho96 / DLM04) values for Ta (V0_lit = 0.018000 nm^3,
  K0_lit = 194 GPa, K0'_lit = 3.83) serve as a reference set against which the fit values
  were compared; the fit values are essentially identical within quoted uncertainties.
doi: 10.1080/09511920500147501
notes:

data_quality_notes: |
  This revised helium-specific ruby scale was constructed so that pressures derived
  from ruby fluorescence match those inferred from the primary MgO scale (Zha et al. 2000)
  during single-crystal MgO compression in He. The previous ruby scale (Mao et al. 1986)
  underestimates pressures by 5–8% above 60–80 GPa in helium environments.

doi: 10.2138/am.2008.2988

notes: |
  This calibration is valid only for experiments performed in helium as a pressure 
  medium. It should not be used for non-He media (Ne, Ar, KCl, MEW, etc.) because the 
  correction is specific to the soft stress environment of He in DACs. Jacobsen et al. 
  proposed this ruby correction to reconcile He-based MgO measurements with the 
  primary MgO scale.

K0_dprime_unc:

K_infinity_prime_unc:

data_quality_notes: Static DAC experiments were conducted under nonhydrostatic conditions (with and without a 4:1 methanol:ethanol medium), leading to significant shear stresses and preferred orientation in Re. The DAC XRD P–V data are systematically biased (volumes too large at a given pressure) relative to a hydrostatic 300 K isotherm.The reported equation of state is a fourth-order Eulerian finite-strain EOS for Re at 300 K, obtained by combining ultrasonic measurements (K0T, K0T') with shock Hugoniot data reduced using a Mie–Grüneisen formalism.
doi: [Static strength and equation of state of rhenium at ultra-high pressures]
notes: |
    This paper investigates the static strength and equation of state of rhenium (Re) at pressures up to ~120 GPa using diamond-anvil cell X-ray diffraction, revealing that rhenium is one of the strongest known polycrystalline metals under extreme compression . By analyzing pressure–volume data alongside ultrasonic and shock-wave measurements, the authors show that a fourth-order equation of state is required to accurately describe Re’s compression behavior, and that nonhydrostatic stresses in experiments lead to systematic deviations in measured volumes. These deviations are used to quantify shear stresses, which reach ~10–15 GPa at high pressures, corresponding to ~4% of the shear modulus—consistent with theoretical expectations.

data_quality_notes: |
  First direct experimental determination of the 300 K EOS of cubic BN
  to ultrahigh pressures. Pressures determined using ruby fluorescence;
  volumes from single diffraction peak (111) due to weak higher-order
  reflections at high pressure. Despite nonhydrostatic effects above
  ~15–20 GPa, the large compression range (to 115 GPa) allows K0 and K0'
  to be constrained with reasonable precision.

doi: 10.1038/337349a0
notes: |
  EOS obtained by fitting static compression data with a third-order
  Birch–Murnaghan equation of state (Eulerian finite strain).
  Reported parameters: K0 = 369 ± 14 GPa, K0' = 4.0 ± 0.2.
  Reference volume V0 corresponds to the ambient-pressure lattice
  parameter a0 = 3.615 ± 0.002 Å.

  Authors demonstrate consistency between this static EOS, first-principles
  calculations, and independent shock Hugoniot data, supporting the
  reliability of the ruby pressure calibration at ultrahigh pressures.
