reference_temperature: 300 K

data_quality_notes: |
  Helium PTM greatly improved hydrostatic conditions. Heat-treated Cu had 
  fewer microstructural defects, yielding more uniform Debye rings and more 
  consistent P–V data.
doi: doi.org/10.1016/j.cap.2021.08.006
notes: |
  Only the heat-treated polycrystalline Cu sample is retained in the EoSAlign
  database because Ko et al. (2021) show that this dataset provides the most
  reliable and internally consistent equation of state. Among the three sample
  types studied (polycrystalline untreated, polycrystalline heat-treated,
  and single crystal), the heat-treated polycrystal exhibited: 
    (1) the most hydrostatic conditions due to helium PTM, 
    (2) the lowest microstructural defect density, 
    (3) the most uniform and symmetric diffraction rings, and 
    (4) the smallest residuals in the Birch–Murnaghan fit.
  The untreated polycrystalline sample shows softening attributed to defect
  populations, and the single-crystal sample shows orientation effects and
  nonuniform intensities. For these reasons, the heat-treated polycrystalline
  Cu dataset is considered the authoritative EOS representation in this study.

  newly determined 298 K isotherm of Cu as an absolute pressure reference.
  The calibration relies on quasi-hydrostatic DAC data from Dewaele et al. (2004),
  with pressure recalculated using the Kraus Cu isotherm.

  The functional form follows the standard ruby power-law:
      P = (A/B) * [ (1 + Δλ/λ0)^B – 1 ]
  where A = 19.151 Mbar and B = 10.603.

  Uncertainty is dominated by the Cu isotherm uncertainty (~3% at 150 GPa)
  and possible nonhydrostatic stress in the referenced DAC measurements.

doi: 10.1103/PhysRevB.93.134105
notes: |
  This ruby calibration supersedes Mao et al. (1986) at high pressure and
  is consistent with Holzapfel (2010) and Chijioke et al. (2005) within
  stated uncertainties. Above ~150 GPa the calibration is unconstrained
  by data and should be treated as extrapolative.

  measurements determine V0 directly and yield the preferred Rydberg–Vinet

    and 4000 K (no evidence for β-Be or bcc-Be).  # Fig. 1 and sections II–III.

    a quasiharmonic Mie–Grüneisen–Debye model; these parameters were

# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Lei et al., 2013(P1)
category: 1
composition: Al2O3  # ruby (Cr-doped α-Al2O3)
method: Luminescence
technique: DAC
DAC_type: not specified
culet: not specified
cal_to_name: Carter et al., 1971; Carter et al., 1971; Mao et al., 1986; Zou et al., 1982
cal_to_composition: Cu; Ag; Cu; Ag
eos: Power
order:
# Quasi-hydrostatic ruby equation (Argon PTM)
# P (GPa) = (A/B) * [ (1 + Δλ/λ0)^B – 1 ]
A: 1904
B: 9.827
lambda_0: 694.24   # nm

max_pressure: 100  # GPa
PTM: Ar #Ar for Cu & Ag; 4:1 methanol–ethanol (ME) for Mo (in the corrected MXB86 datasets)
synchrotron: none
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: R1 luminescence line
reference_temperature: 300 #K
sample_heating: none

data_quality_notes: |
  This study revisits the ruby fluorescence pressure scale by explicitly correcting
  non-hydrostatic stress effects in the DAC X-ray diffraction experiments used by
  Mao et al. (1978, 1986) to calibrate the classic MBSS78 and MXB86 scales.
  The authors model the influence of pressure gradients and differential stress on
  measured d-spacings, expressing the error in d/d0 in terms of the yield strength
  of the pressure transmitting medium (PTM) and the shear modulus of the standard
  material. Using these corrections, they recalculate the Cu and Ag compression
  data in Ar, and Mo in 4:1 methanol–ethanol, then refit the ruby scale with
  functional form I:
    P = (A/B)[(1 + Δλ/λ0)^B - 1].
  The main P1 scale, based on corrected Cu+Ag-in-Ar data, yields A = 1904 GPa
  (fixed) and B = 9.827. A secondary P2 scale from corrected Mo-in-ME (where this data comes from is not cited) data gives
  B = 8.761. The authors show that P1 agrees well with several recent ruby scales
  (DLM04, CNSS05, DO07, DTLM08) below ~100 GPa and therefore regard P1 as the
  preferred revised ruby pressure scale.

doi: 10.1088/1674-1056/22/5/056201
notes: >
  This YAML records the P1 ruby fluorescence pressure scale proposed by Liu et al. (2013),
  which corrects the MXB86 calibration for non-hydrostatic stresses in DAC XRD data and
  refits the standard Mao-form (functional form I). The parameter A = 1904 GPa is inherited
  from earlier ruby scales and held fixed, while B = 9.827 is obtained from corrected Cu
  and Ag compression data in Ar. The alternative P2 scale, based on Mo in 4:1 ME (A = 1904,
  B = 8.761), is not used here but is relevant for assessing systematic uncertainties
  associated with PTM strength and elastic-property mismatches between standards and ruby.

data_quality_notes: >
  The bcc phase of Ta was observed to remain stable up to 133 GPa using energy-dispersive
  synchrotron XRD in a modified Mao–Bell DAC with Ar as the pressure medium. Unit-cell
  volumes were measured and fit with the Vinet EOS, fixing V0 at 36.01 Å^3. The fit yields
  B0 = 192.65 ± 3.08 GPa and B0' = 3.58 ± 0.11. Hydrostaticity was evaluated by (1) the
  normalized d-spacing method for cubic crystals and (2) monitoring the ruby R1–R2 peak
  separation. Differences between (110) and other d-spacings remain <0.1%, indicating a
  well quasi-hydrostatic stress field over the full pressure range. Ruby R1–R2 splitting
  increases only slowly below ~40 GPa and more noticeably above this pressure, likely when
  ruby grains begin to bridge the anvils.
doi: 10.1088/0256-307X/27/1/016402
notes: >
  EOS parameters were obtained with the Vinet form using V0 fixed at 36.01 Å^3 from their
  own low-pressure volume determination. Results are broadly consistent with previous Ta
  XRD studies (Cynn et al. 1999; Dewaele et al. 2004) and with ultrasonic-based B0 ~194 GPa
  when comparing B0' values across datasets.

  synchrotron XRD in a modified Mao–Bell DAC with Ar as the pressure medium. Unit-cell
  volumes were measured and fit with the Vinet EOS, fixing V0 at 36.01 Å^3. The fit yields
  B0 = 192.65 ± 3.08 GPa and B0' = 3.58 ± 0.11. Hydrostaticity was evaluated by (1) the
  normalized d-spacing method for cubic crystals and (2) monitoring the ruby R1–R2 peak
  separation. Differences between (110) and other d-spacings remain <0.1%, indicating a
  well quasi-hydrostatic stress field over the full pressure range. Ruby R1–R2 splitting
  increases only slowly below ~40 GPa and more noticeably above this pressure, likely when
  ruby grains begin to bridge the anvils.
doi: 10.1088/0256-307X/27/1/016402
notes: |
  EOS parameters were obtained with the Vinet form using V0 fixed at 36.01 Å^3 from their
  own low-pressure volume determination. Results are broadly consistent with previous Ta
  XRD studies (Cynn et al. 1999; Dewaele et al. 2004) and with ultrasonic-based B0 ~194 GPa
  when comparing B0' values across datasets.

data_quality_notes: |
   Helium DAC data show high precision but include several key uncertainties. Volume measurements have ~0.4% uncertainty from diffraction angle, energy calibration, and reflection centering, while pressure relies on the quasi-hydrostatic ruby scale, introducing calibration dependence.  Although He provides near-ideal hydrostatic conditions, its very low x-ray scattering leads to signal limitations, and small alignment or positioning errors can affect results. At higher pressures (>20 GPa), increased data scatter and deviations arise from thermal and many-body effects, and phase transitions show hysteresis and crystal break-up, complicating precise phase boundary determination.
doi: 10.1103/PhysRevLett.71.2272
notes: |
    Helium DAC measurements by Loubeyre et al. (1993) use single-crystal synchrotron X-ray diffraction over ~1–58 GPa and 46–400 K, producing high-quality P–V–T data with sharp reflections from in situ grown crystals. The hcp structure is stable across most conditions, with a limited fcc stability loop near melting and strong hysteresis in the fcc–hcp transition, indicating path-dependent phase boundaries and crystal break-up during transformation. The equation of state shows no significant change in c/a ratio but deviates from simple pair-potential models, demonstrating the increasing importance of many-body interactions at high density. Thermal expansion data (95–304 K) further reveal excess thermal pressure relative to theoretical predictions, suggesting non-phonon contributions.

data_quality_notes: |
  Pressure is determined using mixed calibrations (ruby ≤80 GPa, Re gasket >80 GPa), introducing inconsistency at high pressure. At higher P, only a few diffraction peaks are observable and interference from gasket signals and Kossel lines further reduces data quality.

doi: 10.1088/0953-8984/14/41/304
notes: |
  Nakano et al. (2002) present synchrotron powder X-ray diffraction measurements of beryllium up to 171 GPa at room temperature to investigate its structure and equation of state. The results show that Be remains in the hcp structure across the entire pressure range, with no evidence for previously proposed hcp–hexagonal or theoretically predicted hcp–bcc (or fcc) phase transitions. The lattice parameters decrease smoothly with pressure, and the c/a ratio increases slightly toward the ideal value. An equation of state fitted using the Birch–Murnaghan model gives K₀ ≈ 97 GPa and K′₀ ≈ 3.6, consistent with earlier static compression studies but lower than shock-wave results. Overall, the study provides high-pressure structural and compressibility data for Be, though interpretation is limited by non-hydrostatic conditions and mixed pressure calibration methods.

No anomalies or discontinuities in ν(P) were observed in the 8.6–14.5 GPa range, where an hcp → distorted-hcp transition had been previously claimed from XRD. The frequency shift is positive and smooth across the full range.

doi: 10.1088/0953-8984/12/41/317

notes: |
  Equation (1) parameters δ0 and δ0' define the nonlinear ν(P) dependence. Authors relate the TO frequency to the elastic shear modulus C44.They find Be’s unusually low mode Grüneisen parameter arises from a high C44 relative to its bulk modulus. No structural transition is detected by Raman to 23 GPa.

is_K0_fixed: no

K0_prime: 4.72
K0_prime_unc: 0.50

max_pressure: 270
PTM: MgO
gasket: Re
synchrotron: PF BL13A and SPring-8 BL10XU
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: (100), (110), (111)
reference_temperature: 300
sample_heating: in situ laser annealing to remove deviatoric stress (not used for EOS fitting)
data_quality_notes: |
  The B2-type NaCl EOS study by Ono (2010) includes several notable sources of uncertainty. Experimental data are affected by differential stress in the DAC, which requires laser annealing to reduce but may not be fully eliminated, introducing residual non-hydrostatic errors. Pressure determination depends on gold as an internal calibrant combined with revised ruby scales, meaning results are sensitive to the chosen pressure standard and known discrepancies between scales (up to ~5–10% at high pressure). The study also highlights that first-principles calculations carry significant uncertainty, with previously reported bulk moduli differing by up to ~50%, and results depending on computational choices (GGA, PAW, simulation conditions).
doi: 10.1088/1742-6596/215/1/012196
notes: |
  no (2010) investigates the equation of state of B2-type NaCl using a combination of high-pressure diamond anvil cell experiments and first-principles molecular dynamics calculations up to ~270 GPa and 3000 K. Room-temperature experimental data are used to constrain the static EOS, while simulations are used to model thermal pressure and high-temperature behavior. The results are fitted using a Vinet EOS, yielding bulk modulus and pressure derivative values consistent with corrected pressure scales. The study finds that thermal pressure has only weak volume dependence but strong temperature dependence, and that the Grüneisen parameter varies with temperature, indicating significant anharmonic effects. Overall, the work shows that conventional approaches like the Mie–Grüneisen approximation are insufficient for B2 NaCl at high P–T conditions, and that combining experimental and computational methods is necessary for a more reliable EOS.

# --- Pressure determination ---

# --- Full P–T Raman calibration (polynomial form; Eq. 1) ---
# ν_TO(P,T) = ν0 + aT + bT^2 + (c0 + c1 T) P + d P^2
V0: 1059.3                  # cm^-1 (ν0 at 300 K, 0 GPa)
V0_unc: 1.0                 # cm^-1
A: -0.0220                  # cm^-1/K
A_unc:                      # not independently resolved (see notes)
B: -0.00000491              # cm^-1/K^2
B_unc:
C: 3.371                    # cm^-1/GPa (c0)
C_unc: 0.070
D: 0.000119                 # cm^-1/(GPa·K) (c1)
D_unc: 0.000055
E: 0                          # not used in this formulation
E_unc:
F: -0.0076                  # cm^-1/GPa^2
F_unc: 0.0010

max_pressure: 90            # GPa
PTM: NaCl #(pressure medium); Au (pressure marker)
gasket: Re
synchrotron: SPring-8 BL10XU
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: c-BN TO Raman mode
reference_temperature: 300–800   # K
sample_heating: resistive external heating (HDAC)

data_quality_notes: |
  Raman TO-mode shifts of c-BN were measured up to 90 GPa and 800 K.
  Pressure was determined independently from the unit-cell volume
  of gold measured by in situ synchrotron XRD, using a published
  gold equation of state. Ruby fluorescence was not used during
  heating.

  Polynomial fitting parameters follow Eq. (1) of Ono et al. (2015).
  Temperature-dependent coefficients a and b were partially adopted
  from Kawamoto et al. (2004) due to limited sensitivity at ambient
  pressure.

doi: 10.1016/j.jpcs.2014.09.001
notes: |
  This study extends the Raman calibration of the c-BN TO mode to
  significantly higher pressures (90 GPa) than previous work.
  A clear reduction in dν/dP with increasing pressure is observed,
  confirming strong nonlinearity above ~40 GPa.

  Unlike Datchi & Canny (2004), pressure is determined directly from
  an internal gold standard via XRD, making this calibration less
  sensitive to assumptions about ruby behavior at high temperature.

  The polynomial form is valid only within the calibrated P–T range;
  extrapolation beyond 90 GPa is strongly discouraged.

# --- Pressure determination ---
# Pressure derived from NaCl compression using Decker EOS
cal_to_name: Decker 1971  #Also cited Decker 1965/1966
cal_to_composition: NaCl-B1


eos: LinearShift

# --- Linear ruby calibration at 25 °C ---
# P (kbar) = A * Δλ; LinearShift Equation is P=Δλ/Ae
lambda_0: 694.24            # nm (R1 line at 25 °C, ambient pressure)
lambda_0_unc: 0.02           # nm (instrumental uncertainty)

A: 0.36417                  # nm/GPa =2.746 kbar/Å
A_unc:              # 0.014 kbar/Å (95% confidence interval)


max_pressure: 19.5           # 195 kbar (~19.5 GPa)
PTM: ME #methanol–ethanol (4:1)
synchrotron: none (laboratory Mo Kα XRD)
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: ruby R1 fluorescence line; NaCl (200) XRD
reference_temperature: 25   # °C
sample_heating: none

data_quality_notes: |
  Ruby R1 wavelength shifts were calibrated against pressures
  derived from NaCl compression using the Decker equation of
  state. Measurements were hydrostatic to ~104 kbar and
  quasi-hydrostatic from 104–195 kbar. Separate fits for
  hydrostatic and combined datasets yield indistinguishable
  slopes within uncertainty.

  Quadratic terms in Δλ were evaluated but found to be statistically
  insignificant over the full pressure range.

doi: 10.1063/1.321957
notes: |
  Recommended calibration (25 °C):
    P (kbar) = 2.740 · Δλ (Å)

  This calibration defines pressures relative to the Decker EOS
  for NaCl and does not constitute an absolute pressure standard.
  The authors explicitly caution that systematic uncertainty is
  dominated by the NaCl EOS rather than by ruby fluorescence
  measurement precision.

  This study establishes ruby as a secondary pressure standard
  and forms the foundation for later ruby scales (e.g., Mao 1978,
  Mao 1986), which adopt different functional forms and anchors.

  pressure range.

data_quality_notes: |
  Static compression data for solid argon were collected to
  800 kbar at room temperature. Argon remains fcc over the
  entire pressure range. Unit-cell volumes were determined
  from the (111) and (200) reflections.

  Pressure was determined using a ruby fluorescence scale
  calibrated for hydrostatic and quasi-hydrostatic conditions,
  appropriate for weakly bound solids such as Ar.
doi: 10.1063/1.451346
notes: |
  This study does not present a closed-form analytical EOS
  (e.g., Birch–Murnaghan or Vinet) fit to the static Ar data.
  Instead, static P–V measurements are compared directly
  with isotherms reduced from shock Hugoniot data using an
  exp-6 interatomic potential.

  The agreement between static and shock-derived isotherms
  is excellent up to ~400 kbar, supporting the validity of
  the ruby pressure scale at these pressures to within ~10%.

  The authors suggest that solid argon may serve as a static
  pressure standard up to ~3–4 Mbar when combined with
  theoretical reduction of shock data.
Sakai et al., 2011_BM_DO.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Sakai et al., 2011
category: 1
composition: NaCl-B2
method: XRD
technique: DAC
DAC_type: symmetric
culet: 40  #um
cal_to_name: Dorogokupets and Oganov 2007  
cal_to_composition: Pt   
eos: Birch-Murnaghan
order: 3
V0: 37.76     #A^3
V0_unc:
K0: 47.24  #GPa
is_K0_fixed: no
K0_unc: 0.47
K0_prime: 4.14 
K0_prime_unc: 0.02

max_pressure: 304 #GPa
PTM: NaCl
gasket: W
synchrotron: Spring-8, BL10XU
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: B2 (100), (110), (111)
reference_temperature: 300 
sample_heating: double-sided laser annealing to ~1500–2000 K (to minimize stress) 
data_quality_notes: |
  The study presents experimental data extending the NaCl-B2 equation of state to ultra-high pressures (up to 304 GPa), with the B2 (CsCl-type) phase confirmed to remain stable throughout this range. Volume calculations are robustly constrained using three independent diffraction peaks—(100), (110), and (111)—which improves reliability and reduces crystallographic bias. The use of platinum as an internal pressure standard, along with detailed d-spacing measurements for both Pt and NaCl (shown in Fig. 1 on page 3), supports internal consistency. Additionally, the authors carefully address nonhydrostatic stress (estimated ~2.4 GPa) and recalibrate previous datasets (Sata et al.) using multiple Pt pressure scales, which strengthens comparability. However, uncertainties arise from dependence on pressure scale choice and the fixed V0 derived indirectly from the g–G plot, as the B2 phase cannot be quenched to ambient conditions
doi: 10.1063/1.3573393
notes: |
  Overall, the Dorogokupets and Oganov (2007) Pt scale produces only moderate deviations compared to other calibrations, with pressure differences of about +12 GPa (~3.4%) at 364 GPa relative to the Matsui scale. The B–M EoS based on this calibration remains internally consistent and agrees well with experimental compression data across the studied range. Importantly, differences between B–M and Vinet EoS formulations are small (<2%), confirming that the dominant source of uncertainty is the choice of Pt pressure scale rather than the EoS model itself.

data_quality_notes: |
  The study presents experimental data extending the NaCl-B2 equation of state to ultra-high pressures (up to 304 GPa), with the B2 (CsCl-type) phase confirmed to remain stable throughout this range. Volume calculations are robustly constrained using three independent diffraction peaks—(100), (110), and (111)—which improves reliability and reduces crystallographic bias. The use of platinum as an internal pressure standard, along with detailed d-spacing measurements for both Pt and NaCl (shown in Fig. 1 on page 3), supports internal consistency. Additionally, the authors carefully address nonhydrostatic stress (estimated ~2.4 GPa) and recalibrate previous datasets (Sata et al.) using multiple Pt pressure scales, which strengthens comparability. However, uncertainties arise from dependence on pressure scale choice and the fixed V0 derived indirectly from the g–G plot, as the B2 phase cannot be quenched to ambient conditions.
doi: 10.1063/1.3573393
notes: |
  Using the platinum (Pt) pressure scale of Holmes et al. (1989), the NaCl-B2 equation of state fitted with the Vinet formulation shows noticeable deviations compared to more recent calibrations, particularly at extreme pressures. The fitted parameters are: zero-pressure volume V₀ ≈ 38.01 Å³ (fixed), bulk modulus K₀ ≈ 36.07 GPa, and pressure derivative K₀′ ≈ 5.49. These values indicate a relatively softer bulk modulus and a stronger pressure dependence (higher K₀′), leading to steeper curvature under compression. As a result, this calibration produces significantly higher pressure estimates, with differences reaching up to about +37 GPa (~10%) at ~364 GPa compared to more recent Pt scales. Overall, while internally consistent, the Holmes et al. Vinet-based calibration tends to overestimate pressure at multi-megabar conditions, highlighting limitations of older Pt pressure scales for modern high-pressure studies.

The Vinet formulation provides a consistent description of Fe compression at extreme pressures, slightly emphasizing higher curvature (higher K₀′) compared to Birch–Murnaghan fits .

data_quality_notes: | 
  synchrotron XRD, multiple diffraction peaks for volume determination, and laser annealing to reduce deviatoric stress in the DAC. However, uncertainties are strongly influenced by the pressure calibration: the Holmes et al. (1989) Pt scale is known to overestimate pressure, and this bias propagates through the NaCl-B2 calibration, leading to systematic deviations of up to ~10% at multi-megabar pressures. Residual nonhydrostatic stress (~4–5 GPa) can affect volume by ~0.4–0.6%, and weak diffraction peaks (e.g., the hcp 002 reflection) may introduce additional scatter. Furthermore, the choice of the Vinet EoS increases sensitivity in K₀′ compared to Birch–Murnaghan fits. Despite these limitations, the consistency of volume results with other studies indicates that the measurements themselves are robust, with uncertainties dominated by calibration rather than experimental precision. 

doi: 10.1002/2014JB011019
notes: |
   In Sakai et al. (2014), the equation of state of hcp-Fe is determined from synchrotron XRD measurements in a symmetric-type DAC (300–150 µm culets), with pressure calibrated via the NaCl-B2 scale (Sakai et al., 2011) linked to the Holmes et al. (1989) Pt scale. The compression data are fitted using a third-order Rydberg–Vinet equation of state with all parameters free. For this calibration (P6-scale), the fitted parameters are approximately V₀ ≈ 22.21 Å³, K₀ ≈ 167.2 GPa, and K₀′ ≈ 5.93. The dataset extends to ~279 GPa and shows that the Holmes-based calibration yields lower bulk modulus and higher pressure derivative compared to more recent scales, reflecting a softer apparent compression curve but with similar volumes at inner core conditions.

# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Sakai et al., 2018
category: 1
composition: Re   # hcp rhenium
method: XRD
technique: DAC and ds-DAC
DAC_type: conventional DAC; double-stage DAC (ds-DAC)
culet: 3–5 μm 2nd-stage micro-anvils; 40 μm double-beveled primary anvils  # μm

cal_to_name: Yokoo et al., 2009
cal_to_composition: Pt

eos: Rydberg-Vinet
order: 3

V0: 29.47              # Å^3 per hcp unit cell (2 atoms), fixed
V0_unc:

K0: 358                # GPa
K0_unc: 10
is_K0_fixed: no

K0_prime: 4.8          # dimensionless
K0_prime_unc: 0.2


max_pressure: 464      # GPa, maximum Re pressure in the conventional DAC Re+Pt experiment (Run RP01)
PTM: glycerin; silica glass
gasket: Re (ds-DAC); W (conventional DAC)
synchrotron: SPring-8 BL10XU
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: hcp Re (100), (002), (101)   # 101 used for highest-pressure deconvolution

reference_temperature: 300   # K
sample_heating: none

data_quality_notes: >
  Rhenium was used both as a sample and as a pressure marker in double-stage DAC (ds-DAC)
  experiments with nano-polycrystalline diamond (NPD) 2nd-stage anvils, and in a conventional
  DAC experiment to 280 GPa with Pt as a co-loaded sample. Pressures in the ds-DAC runs were
  calculated from Re unit-cell volumes using the Re EOS of Anzellini et al. (2014). In the
  conventional DAC experiment (Run RP01), Re and Pt were compressed together with a silica
  glass pressure medium; pressures from the Pt EOS of Yokoo et al. (2009) and Dewaele et al.
  (2004) were compared.

  To examine consistency between Pt and Re scales, Sakai et al. refit their Re compression data
  based on the Yokoo et al. (2009) Pt scale with a Vinet EOS, fixing V0 = 29.47 Å^3 (hcp unit
  cell), obtaining K0 = 358(10) GPa and K0' = 4.8(2). This EOS yields maximum ds-DAC pressures
  of ~464 GPa for V = 18.65 Å^3 (V/V0 = 0.633), whereas the Re EOS of Dubrovinsky et al. (2012)
  would give ~630 GPa for the same volume, which Sakai et al. argue is unreasonably high.

  XRD peaks from Re in ds-DAC runs are very broad due to extreme pressure gradients within the
  ~3 μm X-ray beam footprint; the highest-pressure component is extracted by deconvolving the
  Re 101 peak into multiple Lorentzians. Conventional DAC data show sharper peaks (FWHM ~0.2°),
  providing the main constraint for the Vinet fit.

doi: 10.1080/08957959.2018.1448082

notes: |
  This YAML encodes the Re Vinet EOS (V0 fixed at 29.47 Å^3, K0 = 358 GPa, K0' = 4.8) derived
  by Sakai et al. (2018) from combined Re+Pt compression data calibrated against the Yokoo
  et al. (2009) Pt pressure scale. It is intended for use as an alternative high-pressure Re
  EOS in ds-DAC contexts, particularly for pressures above ~200 GPa where discrepancies between
  different Re scales become significant. Sakai et al. conclude that the Re EOS of Dubrovinsky
  et al. (2012) substantially overestimates pressures in the 400–600 GPa range relative to Pt-
  based calibrations.

Schulte and Holzapfel 1996_H12.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Schulte and Holzapfel 1996
category: 1
composition: Zn
method: XRD
technique: DAC
DAC_type: 
culet: 300  #um
cal_to_name: Mao et al., 1978  #separate by a ; if multiple references are used 
cal_to_composition: Al2O3 #separate by a ; if multiple compositions are used  

eos: H12
V0_per_atom: 15.22      # Å^3/atom; V0(Zn) = 0.01522 nm^3 from Donohue (1974), cited in the paper 
V0_unc:
K0: 63        # GPa this study only 
is_K0_fixed: no

K0_unc: 2
K0_prime: 5.2    
K0_prime_unc: 0.7
atomic_number: 30 

max_pressure: 74     # GPa; highest Zn pressure reached in this study 
PTM: Liquid N2; mineral oil  # both used, no notable differences reported
gasket: Inconel X750 
synchrotron: HASYLAB DESY, EDS station F3; plus laboratory W-anode tube 
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: Five hP2 Zn reflections (five dhkl values used to refine a and c at each pressure)
reference_temperature: 300 K
sample_heating: none

data_quality_notes: |
  Zinc was studied up to 74 GPa in the hP2 structure with energy-dispersive XRD.
  Pressures were measured using ruby luminescence on the Mao et al. (1978) nonlinear scale.
  The axial ratio c/a decreases monotonically with pressure and crosses the ideal hcp value
  (c/a = 1.633) near V/V0 ≈ 0.74. Previous low-pressure XRD anomalies in c/a are attributed
  to deviatoric stress; the present data show only a smooth decrease. The authors compare
  multiple EOS forms (MU2, BE2, MV2, H02, H12, H11) for Zn and conclude that the simple,
  one-parameter Holzapfel H11 form gives an excellent description of the combined static,
  shock, and ultrasonic data, with K0 ≈ 61 GPa and an implied K0′ ≈ 5.5.

doi: 10.1103/PhysRevB.53.569

notes: |
  This YAML records the Zn EOS using the Holzapfel H11 form, which the authors identify as
  the most appropriate "simple" EOS for Zn when all available p–V data (static DAC, volumetric,
  and shock-wave) are considered together. In H11, only K0 is an independent parameter; K0′ is
  determined by the correlation K0′ = 3 - (2/3) ln(3K0 / pFG0), where pFG0 is the Fermi-gas
  limiting pressure at V0. For Zn, this yields K0′ ≈ 5.5 for K0 ≈ 61 GPa. Alternative two-parameter
  fits (MU2, BE2, MV2, H02, H12) give similar K0–K0′ pairs but diverge more strongly at extreme
  compression. Zn behaves as a "soft" simple solid relative to the ideal Thomas–Fermi-like
  asymptotic behavior in h–X plots, consistent with its unusual c/a and d-band contributions.
Shen and Smith _2026_Au_1.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated May 2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Shen and Smith 2026
category: 1
composition: Au
method: XRD
technique: DAC
DAC_type: symmetric-type DAC 
culet: 150 um; beveled to 300 um 
cal_to_name: Fratanduono et al., 2020  #separate by a ; if multiple references are used 
cal_to_composition: Cu #separate by a ; if multiple compositions are used  
eos: Rydberg-Vinet
order: 3
V0: 67.792
V0_unc: #fixed
K0: 167.5   # GPa
is_K0_fixed: no
K0_unc: 0.3
K0_prime: 5.85
K0_prime_unc: 0.01

max_pressure: 140 # GPa
PTM: He # quasihydrostatic 
gasket: Re 
synchrotron: HPCAT 16-ID-B; APS; ANL 
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: fcc (111), (220), (222), (311)  
reference_temperature: 300 # K
sample_heating: # not specified
data_quality_notes: |
    The gold (Au) dataset exhibits high overall precision, though with slightly more variability compared to Pt due to experimental factors. Diffraction peaks remain sharp throughout compression to ~140 GPa, allowing reliable extraction of lattice parameters with typical relative volume uncertainties of ~0.05–0.1% . Multiple fcc reflections [(111), (220), (222), and (311)] were used to improve accuracy and reduce sensitivity to stress anisotropy. Small discrepancies are observed between the two experimental runs (DAC-1 and DAC-2), primarily attributed to differences in grain size and initial loading conditions. In particular, the Au grain in DAC-2 showed signs of increased deviatoric stress and possible partial bridging, leading to slightly higher scatter in the measured volumes. Lattice-strain analysis confirms that Au experiences somewhat higher stress in DAC-2 relative to DAC-1, though still within acceptable limits for quasihydrostatic conditions. Consequently, the DAC-1 dataset, which shows lower scatter, is selected for primary analysis. Despite these minor variations, the Au data remain internally consistent, with deviations well within experimental uncertainty.
    
doi: 10.1103/fxgq-96sg  
notes: | 
    Gold (Au) is a widely used pressure calibrant and demonstrates consistent, well-characterized compressional behavior in this study. The experimentally determined volume–volume (V–V) relations show strong agreement with prior literature and confirm Au’s reliability as a secondary standard when referenced to Cu . The derived equation of state (EOS) parameters (K₀ ≈ 167.5 GPa, K′₀ ≈ 5.85) closely match previous static and dynamic measurements, reinforcing confidence in its calibration performance. However, comparison with ramp-derived EOS models reveals a small systematic negative deviation (ΔV/V ≈ −0.4%), suggesting that the dynamic Au EOS predicts slightly larger volumes (i.e., a softer response) relative to the Cu-referenced scale. Despite this minor offset, the discrepancy remains small and within the broader uncertainty range of high-pressure EOS comparisons. DAC-1 was used for fitting.

data_quality_notes: |
    The magnesium oxide (MgO) dataset shows good overall quality with reliable measurements across the full pressure range, though with slightly larger uncertainty compared to some metallic calibrants. Diffraction peaks remain well-defined up to ~140 GPa, allowing extraction of lattice parameters with typical relative volume uncertainties of ~0.1% . Unit-cell volumes were determined using the (200) and (220) reflections, which provide sufficient constraint while minimizing peak overlap. Data were obtained from DAC-2, where MgO was co-loaded with other calibrants in a helium pressure medium, ensuring quasihydrostatic conditions. Deviatoric stress effects are present but remain small, contributing only minor systematic uncertainty. Compared to metals like Pt or Mo, MgO exhibits slightly greater scatter and sensitivity to stress due to its ionic structure, but the dataset remains internally consistent. Overall, the MgO data are of solid quality, with manageable uncertainties and no major anomalies.
    
doi: 10.1103/fxgq-96sg  
notes: | 
    Magnesium oxide (MgO) serves as an important ionic pressure calibrant and displays consistent compressional behavior in this study. The experimentally derived volume–volume (V–V) relations relative to Cu are smooth and well-defined, though comparisons with previous studies show larger discrepancies (exceeding ~1% in volume in some cases), suggesting greater sensitivity to experimental conditions or calibration methods . The fitted equation of state (EOS) parameters (K₀ ≈ 161.9 GPa, K′₀ ≈ 4.08) are generally consistent with prior work, supporting its validity as a secondary standard. In terms of stiffness scaling, MgO behaves similarly to bcc metals, showing moderate increases in bulk modulus with compression. While slightly less precise than some metallic calibrants, MgO remains a useful and widely applied standard, particularly for comparisons involving oxide materials. For fitting, DAC-2 was used.

data_quality_notes: |
   The molybdenum (Mo) dataset demonstrates high precision and strong internal consistency across the full pressure range investigated. Diffraction peaks remain sharp and well-defined up to ~140 GPa, enabling accurate determination of lattice parameters with typical relative volume uncertainties of ~0.05–0.1% . Multiple bcc reflections were used to calculate unit-cell volumes, reducing sensitivity to peak overlap and improving robustness. Measurements from both DAC-1 and DAC-2 runs show excellent agreement, with relative differences generally <0.1%, indicating minimal influence from spatial pressure gradients or sample positioning. Deviatoric stress effects are small under helium quasihydrostatic conditions, contributing only minor systematic uncertainty. Overall, the Mo dataset is dense, low-scatter, and reproducible, making it one of the more reliable datasets in the study.
    
doi: 10.1103/fxgq-96sg  
notes: | 
    Molybdenum (Mo) exhibits stable and well-characterized compressional behavior, confirming its suitability as a high-pressure calibrant. The experimentally derived volume–volume (V–V) relations relative to Cu are smooth and consistent, reflecting reliable relative compressibility across the studied pressure range . The fitted equation of state (EOS) parameters (K₀ ≈ 260.6 GPa, K′₀ ≈ 4.06) are in close agreement with previous experimental and theoretical results, reinforcing confidence in the dataset. Comparisons with literature show good agreement at lower pressures, though small discrepancies (up to ~1% in volume) emerge at higher compression when compared to some neon-medium studies, suggesting minor medium- or methodology-dependent differences. Mo’s bulk modulus scaling behavior is consistent with other bcc metals, showing moderate stiffening with compression. For fitting, DAC-1 and DAC-2 combined.

order: 3

K0: 23.5   # GPa
is_K0_fixed: no

K0_prime_unc: 0.06

max_pressure: 31 # GPa, phase transition up to 31GPa
PTM: He # quasihydrostatic 
gasket: Re 
synchrotron: HPCAT 16-ID-B; APS; ANL 
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: (200), (220) 
reference_temperature: 300 # K
sample_heating: # not specified
data_quality_notes: |
   The NaCl-B1 (rocksalt) dataset shows moderate-to-good data quality within its stability range, with some limitations tied to phase behavior and fitting constraints. Diffraction peaks remain identifiable and usable up to ~30–31 GPa, allowing determination of unit-cell volumes with typical relative uncertainties on the order of ~0.1% . Volumes were derived using the (200) and (220) reflections, which provide sufficient constraint while minimizing peak overlap. A key limitation arises in the B1–B2 coexistence region (~30–35 GPa), where both phases exhibit inconsistent volume trends; data from this region are therefore excluded from fits to maintain accuracy. Additionally, extrapolation to zero pressure is not well constrained by the experimental dataset alone, so the reference volume (V₀) is fixed using literature values during fitting. Despite these constraints, the remaining dataset shows reasonable internal consistency under quasihydrostatic helium conditions, with only minor contributions from deviatoric stress. Overall, NaCl-B1 data are reliable within the single-phase region but require careful handling near the phase transition and at low-pressure extrapolation.
    
doi: 10.1103/fxgq-96sg  
notes: | 
    NaCl-B1 serves as a commonly used ionic pressure calibrant at lower pressures and demonstrates expected compressional behavior within its stability field. The experimentally derived volume–volume (V–V) relations relative to Cu are well-defined in the single-phase region and reflect consistent relative compressibility . The fitted equation of state (EOS) parameters (K₀ ≈ 23.5 GPa, K′₀ ≈ 5.23) align reasonably well with previous studies, confirming its general reliability. However, NaCl-B1 exhibits strong compressibility and increased sensitivity to pressure conditions compared to metallic calibrants, and discrepancies with other datasets can arise, particularly near the B1–B2 transition. Its relatively low bulk modulus and steep compression at low pressures also require more flexible fitting approaches. For fitting, DAC-2 used.

peaks_used_to_fit_sample: (110), (211) 
reference_temperature: 300 # K
sample_heating: # not specified
data_quality_notes: |
   The NaCl-B2 dataset shows good quality within the high-pressure phase field, though with some limitations related to phase transition behavior and structural effects. Diffraction peaks remain identifiable at pressures above ~33 GPa, enabling determination of unit-cell volumes with typical relative uncertainties of ~0.1% . Volumes were calculated using the (110) and (211) reflections, which are appropriate for the B2 structure. Data in the B1–B2 coexistence region (~30–35 GPa) exhibit inconsistent trends and are excluded from fitting to maintain accuracy. Compared to lower-pressure phases and metallic calibrants, NaCl-B2 shows slightly broader diffraction peaks, indicating modest increases in strain and/or grain-size effects at high pressure. Additionally, because NaCl-B2 is not stable at ambient conditions, its zero-pressure volume (V₀) is constrained using literature values during EOS fitting. Despite these factors, the dataset is internally consistent within the single-phase region and benefits from quasihydrostatic conditions provided by the helium medium. Overall, NaCl-B2 data are reliable at high pressures but require careful treatment near the phase transition and in parameter constraints.
    
doi: 10.1103/fxgq-96sg  
notes: | 
    NaCl-B2 represents the high-pressure phase of sodium chloride and provides useful calibration data in the upper pressure regime. The experimentally derived volume–volume (V–V) relations relative to Cu are well-defined in the B2 phase and allow characterization of its compressional behavior independent of an assumed pressure scale . The fitted equation of state (EOS) parameters (K₀ ≈ 24.9 GPa, K′₀ ≈ 5.63) are broadly consistent with prior experimental results, though some discrepancies exceeding ~1% in volume are observed when compared to other studies, reflecting sensitivity to experimental conditions and calibration pathways. NaCl-B2 exhibits higher stiffness than the B1 phase but remains significantly more compressible than metallic calibrants. Its bulk modulus scaling behavior is comparable to other ionic materials and some bcc metals. For fitting, DAC-2 used.

data_quality_notes: | 
    The platinum (Pt) dataset demonstrates high precision and reliability across the full pressure range investigated. Diffraction peaks for Pt remain sharp and well-defined up to ~140 GPa, enabling accurate determination of lattice parameters with very low uncertainty (on the order of ~0.05–0.1% in volume) . The use of multiple reflections [(111), (220), (222), and (311)] further improves robustness by minimizing sensitivity to deviatoric stress effects. Comparison between independent experimental runs (DAC-1 and DAC-2) shows minor differences, primarily attributed to variations in grain size or initial loading conditions rather than systematic error. Pt exhibits very low deviatoric stress (t < 0.5 GPa), corresponding to a negligible volume bias (<0.2%), confirming near-hydrostatic conditions in the helium medium . The selected dataset (DAC-2) shows lower scatter and is therefore used for primary analysis, though inclusion of both runs does not significantly alter the fitted relations. Overall, the Pt data are internally consistent, reproducible, and among the highest-quality measurements in the study.
    
doi: 10.1103/fxgq-96sg 

notes: | 
    Platinum (Pt) serves as a key metallic pressure calibrant and exhibits stable, well-behaved compression under high-pressure conditions. In this study, Pt shows strong agreement with other calibrants through directly measured volume–volume (V–V) relations, indicating consistent relative compressibility when referenced to Cu . The derived equation of state (EOS) parameters for Pt (K₀ ≈ 261 GPa, K′₀ ≈ 5.75) align closely with prior experimental and ramp-compression studies, supporting its reliability as a secondary standard. Additionally, cross-comparisons with dynamic EOS models show that Pt has negligible residual differences (ΔV/V ≈ 0), indicating excellent consistency with the Cu reference scale and reinforcing its role as a benchmark material. Its high bulk modulus and strong stiffening behavior under compression further highlight its suitability for high-pressure calibration work. Overall, Pt is confirmed as a robust, precise, and internally consistent calibrant within the experimental framework. For fitting, DAC-2 used.

data_quality_notes: | 
    The tantalum (Ta) dataset shows notable variability and requires careful selection due to anomalous behavior observed in one experimental run. While diffraction data generally extend to high pressures with measurable peaks, the DAC-2 Ta dataset exhibits significant scatter, broadened diffraction peaks, and an anomalously stiff compression trend relative to both DAC-1 and literature values . This behavior is attributed to likely helium incorporation or defect-related lattice effects, supported by separate heating experiments in which Ta showed irreversible lattice expansion and abnormal thermal response compared to co-loaded materials. As a result, the DAC-2 Ta data are excluded from the primary analysis. In contrast, the DAC-1 dataset demonstrates more consistent behavior, with reasonable agreement with prior studies and acceptable scatter within experimental uncertainty. Although deviatoric stress effects are present, they remain within the quasihydrostatic regime of the helium medium. Overall, the reliability of the Ta dataset depends strongly on dataset selection, with DAC-1 providing usable, moderate-quality data and DAC-2 identified as compromised.
    
doi: 10.1103/fxgq-96sg  

notes: |
    Tantalum (Ta) exhibits more complex and less stable behavior under high-pressure conditions compared to other metallic calibrants in this study. The accepted DAC-1 data produce a consistent volume–volume (V–V) relationship relative to Cu, though with slightly greater uncertainty than materials like Pt or Au . The derived equation of state (EOS) parameters (K₀ ≈ 195 GPa, K′₀ ≈ 3.62) are broadly consistent with previous experimental results, supporting its general validity as a pressure calibrant. However, cross-comparison with ramp-derived EOS models reveals a modest, pressure-dependent positive deviation (ΔV/V up to ~+0.4%), indicating some inconsistency with the Cu-referenced scale at intermediate pressures. The anomalous behavior observed in DAC-2 highlights Ta’s sensitivity to experimental conditions, particularly possible interactions with helium, which may affect lattice properties. Overall, while Ta can serve as a useful calibrant, its data quality is more condition-dependent, and it requires careful validation compared to more robust standards like Pt or Au. For fiitng, DAC-1 used and DAC-2 excluded.

    The tungsten (W) dataset demonstrates strong overall quality and consistency across the full pressure range, with only minor limitations at low pressures. Diffraction peaks for W remain sharp and well-resolved up to ~140 GPa, supporting precise lattice parameter determination with typical relative volume uncertainties on the order of ~0.1% . Unit-cell volumes were derived from multiple bcc reflections, improving robustness and reducing sensitivity to peak overlap. To address this, the zero-pressure volume (V₀) was constrained using literature values during fitting. Across higher pressures, the dataset shows minimal scatter and excellent agreement between DAC-1 and DAC-2 runs, indicating negligible sensitivity to spatial positioning or pressure gradients within the sample chamber. Deviatoric stress effects remain small under helium quasihydrostatic conditions, contributing only minor systematic uncertainty. Overall, aside from low-pressure overlap effects, the W dataset is high quality, internally consistent, and reliable for EOS analysis.

The experimentally derived volume–volume (V–V) relations show strong agreement with previous measurements, particularly those obtained in helium media, reinforcing consistency across independent datasets . The fitted equation of state (EOS) parameters (K₀ ≈ 307 GPa, K′₀ ≈ 4.02) are consistent with literature values and reflect W’s high stiffness and moderate rate of stiffening under compression. Compared to other materials, W shows relatively small deviations when cross-compared with existing calibrations, indicating good internal consistency within the Cu-referenced framework. Its bulk modulus scaling behavior also aligns with other bcc metals, showing a more gradual increase in stiffness relative to close-packed metals like Pt or Au. Overall, W is confirmed as a robust and dependable calibrant with high data quality, minimal systematic issues, and strong agreement with prior high-pressure studies. For fitting, DAC-1 and DAC-2 combined.

K0: 167  #GPa

sample_heating: 
data_quality_notes: |
  They constructed P–V–T equation of state (EoS) for gold by combining quasi-hydrostatic compression data (using helium as a pressure medium) with shock-wave (Hugoniot) data, significantly improving data reliability compared to earlier non-hydrostatic studies. The use of helium minimizes deviatoric stress, and multiple diagnostics (e.g., diffraction peak consistency) confirm near-hydrostatic conditions, enhancing confidence in the 300 K isotherm (page 2–3). However, uncertainties remain due to reliance on the ruby pressure scale (~1% uncertainty), assumptions in the Mie–Grüneisen–Debye model, and limitations in low-compression Hugoniot data where shear strength effects distort results.
doi: 10.1016/S0012-821X(02)00917-2
notes: |
  Data from Takemura et al 2001 were fit. This paper develops an improved gold EoS by jointly inverting static compression and shock-wave data, yielding key parameters such as K₀′ ≈ 5.0 and Grüneisen parameter q ≈ 1.0, and incorporating both phonon and electronic effects. The resulting model provides a more accurate pressure scale, particularly at high P–T conditions relevant to Earth’s mantle. Compared to previous EoSs, the new formulation better reproduces shock data and reduces systematic errors, shifting calculated pressures by about +1 GPa at 660 km depth. Despite this improvement, a remaining ~1.5 GPa discrepancy persists between experimental phase boundaries and seismic observations, indicating that uncertainties in temperature measurements and other experimental factors are still significant.

Smith et al., 2018_Fe.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Smith et al., 2018
category: 2
composition: Fe-hcp
method: XRD #Ramp Compression
technique: Laser-driven ramp compression with VISAR + Lagrangian sound-speed analysis

cal_to_name: Absolute
cal_to_composition: Fe-hcp

eos: Rydberg-Vinet
order: 3

V0: 22.00 # Å^3/unit cell  corresponding to rho0 = 8.43 g/cm^3 for epsilon-Fe
V0_unc:

K0: 177.7   # GPa
is_K0_fixed: no
K0_unc: 0.6

K0_prime: 5.64
K0_prime_unc: 0.01

max_pressure: 1400   # GPa (1.4 TPa)

PTM: none #(dynamic loading)

synchrotron: National Ignition Facility (NIF)

reference_temperature:
sample_heating: >
  Near-isentropic ramp compression from an initial 60 GPa shock state.
  Thermal corrections applied for:
    - initial 60 GPa shock heating
    - plastic work heating from strength
    - deviatoric stress offset.
  Estimated sample temperatures remain substantially below the Hugoniot
  and within the solid hcp-Fe stability field during compression.

data_quality_notes: >
  Iron was precompressed into the hcp phase using an initial 60 GPa shock
  prior to ramp loading. Pressure-density path determined from free-surface
  velocity histories using a Lagrangian sound-speed analysis. Final isentrope
  corrected for strength and thermal effects. The resulting EOS extends to
  1.4 TPa and ~20 g/cm^3, corresponding to estimated core pressures of
  ~3.5-4 Earth-mass rocky exoplanets.

doi: 10.1038/s41550-018-0437-9

notes: |
  The isentropic compression curve was fit using a third-order
  Rydberg-Vinet equation of state:

  P(X)=3K0X^-2(1-X)exp[(1.5K0'-1.5)(1-X)]

  where:
    X = (rho0/rho)^(1/3)
    rho0 = 8.43 g/cm^3 for epsilon-Fe.

  Best-fit parameters reported in the text:
    K0 = 177.7 ± 0.6 GPa
    K0' = 5.64 ± 0.01

  The study states that extrapolation of the fitted EOS agrees with
  first-principles calculations predicting hcp-Fe stability to ~6 TPa.

  The low-pressure release isentrope and initial shocked state
  analysis used SESAME EOS table 2150 for Fe (Kerley, 1993).

  High-pressure strength corrections were constrained using:
    Smith et al., 2013
    Gleason and Mao, 2013

doi: Self-consistent pressure scales based on the equations of state
  for ruby, diamond, MgO, B2–NaCl, as well as Au, Pt, and other metals
  to 4 Mbar and 3000 K
notes: |
  absrtact: Based on the modified formalism of Dorogokupets and Oganov (2007), we calculated the equation of state for diamond, MgO, Ag, Al,
  Au, Cu, Mo, Nb, Pt, Ta, and W by simultaneous optimization of the data of shock-wave experiments and ultrasonic, X-ray diffraction,
  dilatometric, and thermochemical measurements in the temperature range from ~100 K to the melting points and pressures of up to several
  Mbar, depending on the material. The obtained room-temperature isotherms were adjusted with a shift of the R1 luminescence line of ruby,
  which was measured simultaneously with the unit cell parameters of metals in the helium and argon pressure media. The new ruby scale is
  expressed as P(GPa) = 1870⋅Δλ / λ0(1 + 6⋅Δλ / λ0). It can be used for correction of room-pressure isotherms of metals, diamond, and periclase.
  New simultaneous measurements of the volumes of Au, Pt, MgO, and B2-NaCl were used for interrelated test of obtained equations of state
  and calculation of the room-pressure isotherm for B2-NaCl. Therefore, the constructed equations of state for nine metals, diamond, periclase,
  and B2-NaCl can be considered self-consistent and consistent with the ruby scale and are close to a thermodynamic equilibrium. The calculated
  PVT relations can be used as self-consistent pressure scales in the study of the PVT properties of minerals using diamond anvil cell in a wide
  range of temperatures and pressures.

doi: Self-consistent pressure scales based on the equations of state
  for ruby, diamond, MgO, B2–NaCl, as well as Au, Pt, and other metals
  to 4 Mbar and 3000 K
notes: |
  absrtact: Based on the modified formalism of Dorogokupets and Oganov (2007), we calculated the equation of state for diamond, MgO, Ag, Al,
  Au, Cu, Mo, Nb, Pt, Ta, and W by simultaneous optimization of the data of shock-wave experiments and ultrasonic, X-ray diffraction,
  dilatometric, and thermochemical measurements in the temperature range from ~100 K to the melting points and pressures of up to several
  Mbar, depending on the material. The obtained room-temperature isotherms were adjusted with a shift of the R1 luminescence line of ruby,
  which was measured simultaneously with the unit cell parameters of metals in the helium and argon pressure media. The new ruby scale is
  expressed as P(GPa) = 1870⋅Δλ / λ0(1 + 6⋅Δλ / λ0). It can be used for correction of room-pressure isotherms of metals, diamond, and periclase.
  New simultaneous measurements of the volumes of Au, Pt, MgO, and B2-NaCl were used for interrelated test of obtained equations of state
  and calculation of the room-pressure isotherm for B2-NaCl. Therefore, the constructed equations of state for nine metals, diamond, periclase,
  and B2-NaCl can be considered self-consistent and consistent with the ruby scale and are close to a thermodynamic equilibrium. The calculated
  PVT relations can be used as self-consistent pressure scales in the study of the PVT properties of minerals using diamond anvil cell in a wide
  range of temperatures and pressures.

  which was measured simultaneously with the unit cell parameters of metals in the helium and argon pressure media. The new ruby scale is
  expressed as P(GPa) = 1870⋅Δλ / λ0(1 + 6⋅Δλ / λ0). It can be used for correction of room-pressure isotherms of metals, diamond, and periclase.
  New simultaneous measurements of the volumes of Au, Pt, MgO, and B2-NaCl were used for interrelated test of obtained equations of state
  and calculation of the room-pressure isotherm for B2-NaCl. Therefore, the constructed equations of state for nine metals, diamond, periclase,
  and B2-NaCl can be considered self-consistent and consistent with the ruby scale and are close to a thermodynamic equilibrium. The calculated
  PVT relations can be used as self-consistent pressure scales in the study of the PVT properties of minerals using diamond anvil cell in a wide
  range of temperatures and pressures.

data_quality_notes: |
  compression data for MgO under quasi-hydrostatic conditions (using helium up to 52 GPa), with very low lattice parameter uncertainty (~0.03%), demonstrating strong experimental control (page 1, 3). However, data quality limitations arise from inconsistencies between different datasets and methods, particularly between static compression and shock-wave (Hugoniot) data. The assumption of a constant Grüneisen parameter volume dependence (q) is shown to be inadequate, as it cannot simultaneously fit low-pressure static data and high-pressure shock data (pages 5–6). Additional uncertainties stem from pressure calibration differences (e.g., Au vs. NaCl standards showing up to ~6% discrepancy at high P–T, page 4), extrapolation of equations of state beyond measured ranges, and sensitivity to thermal parameters such as heat capacity and temperature estimates. These issues highlight that even with high-quality measurements, model assumptions and calibration choices remain major sources of systematic error .
doi: 10.1029/2000JB900318
notes: |
   This paper develops an improved P–V–T equation of state for MgO by combining precise quasi-hydrostatic compression data with existing high-temperature and shock-wave datasets within a Birch–Murnaghan–Debye framework. The study determines key parameters (e.g., K₀′ ≈ 3.99, γ ≈ 1.524) and shows that a single constant-q model is insufficient; instead, the Grüneisen parameter must decrease with compression to reconcile static and shock data. The resulting model successfully describes MgO behavior from ambient conditions up to ~200 GPa and ~3600 K, providing a unified framework consistent with both experimental and theoretical constraints.

data_quality_notes: |
  helium pressure medium, multiple independent experimental runs, and detailed analysis of stress effects via diffraction peak behavior and ruby fluorescence. However, the study shows that even helium becomes nonhydrostatic above ~30 GPa, leading to measurable uniaxial stresses that can bias lattice parameters depending on crystallographic direction. The magnitude and even sign of stress vary between runs, introducing systematic uncertainty. Additional uncertainties arise from pressure calibration (different ruby scales can shift results by ~5–6% at 100 GPa) and from assumptions in stress correction (e.g., elastic parameters and model dependence). 
doi: 10.1103/PhysRevB.78.104119
notes: | 
    Takemura and Dewaele (2008) present a high-precision isothermal equation of state for gold (Au) based on powder XRD measurements in a DAC using a helium pressure medium, extending to a maximum pressure of 123 GPa. The study emphasizes minimizing stress effects by selecting the (111) diffraction line, which is least sensitive to uniaxial stress, and fitting the resulting P–V data to a Vinet EoS with a fixed bulk modulus B₀ = 167 GPa. The resulting pressure derivative is B₀′ ≈ 5.5 using the conventional ruby scale, and ≈5.9 when using an updated pressure calibration, aligning well with ultrasonic and theoretical values. The work demonstrates that discrepancies in previous Au EoSs largely arise from stress effects and calibration differences rather than intrinsic material behavior .

data_quality_notes: |
  Helium pressure medium, synchrotron XRD (ESRF ID27), and careful experimental design including multiple runs and stress analysis. However, the study highlights that nonhydrostatic stress develops above ~30 GPa even in He, leading to uniaxial stresses that affect lattice spacings depending on crystallographic orientation. The choice to use the (111) reflection minimizes this bias, but residual uncertainties remain. Additional systematic uncertainty arises from pressure calibration, as different ruby scales can shift pressures by ~5–6% at ~100 GPa. Variations in stress state between runs (including both positive and negative uniaxial stress) further contribute to scatter. Overall, the measurements are precise, but accuracy is primarily limited by stress effects and pressure scale calibration rather than instrumental resolution.
doi: 10.1103/PhysRevB.78.104119 
notes: |
    Takemura and Dewaele (2008) determined the equation of state of Au using XRD measurements in a membrane DAC (100–300 µm culets) with a He pressure medium, reaching pressures up to ~131 GPa. The pressure scale is evaluated relative to updated calibrations (including Dorogokupets and Oganov, 2007), and the data are fitted using a third-order Rydberg–Vinet EoS with the bulk modulus fixed at K₀ = 167 GPa. The resulting parameters are V₀ ≈ 16.962 Å³ per atom and K₀′ ≈ 5.9 when using the revised pressure scale, in excellent agreement with ultrasonic and theoretical constraints. The study demonstrates that when stress effects are minimized (by using the (111) reflection), Au provides a reliable and internally consistent pressure standard at high pressures.

doi: 10.1029/2008JB005813
notes: |
  Tange et al. (2009) present a unified and self-consistent P–V–T equation of state for MgO based on multiple pressure-scale-independent datasets, including XRD, elastic (Kₛ), thermal expansion, and shock compression data. The 300 K isotherm is described using a third-order Birch–Murnaghan EoS with parameters V₀ ≈ 74.698 Å³, K₀ ≈ 160.64 GPa, and K₀′ ≈ 4.221, while thermal effects are incorporated through a Mie–Grüneisen–Debye framework. The model successfully reproduces experimental data up to ~200 GPa (and extrapolated toward ~300 GPa) and high temperatures with high accuracy (RMS pressure residual ~0.8 GPa). Importantly, this EoS is developed as a primary pressure scale, independent of other calibrants, and is widely applicable for high-pressure and high-temperature experiments .

  and magnetic results show that the inversion in the pressure derivative
  of c/a (around V/V0 ≈ 0.76) is linked to magnetism, not to the hcp–fcc
  transition. The hcp phase remains stable to at least 160 GPa under
  quasihydrostatic conditions.

data_quality_notes: |
  The AP2 EOS parameters B0 = 125.7 GPa, B0' = 2.921, and C0 = 0.6465 were obtained by
  reducing Be shock Hugoniot data (McQueen et al., 1970; Nellis et al., 1988) to a
  300 K isotherm and fitting with an AP2 form. The resulting shock-reduced EOS is
  significantly stiffer than the static DAC EOS and begins to deviate from it above
  ~20 GPa. The authors attribute the discrepancy to uncertainties in the Grüneisen
  parameter and other assumptions in the shock reduction and emphasize that the
  shock-based EOS should not be used interchangeably with the static one for
  quasi-static compression studies.

data_quality_notes: |
  The Cu EOS recorded here is not based on new Cu experiments in Velisavljević et al. (2002),
  but on an AP2-form fit to existing Cu shock Hugoniot data. The authors state that fitting the
  Cu shock data to the AP2-EOS yields K0 = 133 GPa and K0' = 5.4, and that these parameters
  were used for pressure determination when Cu served as the internal standard in their Be
  diamond-anvil cell experiments. The AP2 c0 parameter is constrained by K0, V0, and the
  atomic number Z via the Fermi-gas constant a_FG and is not explicitly tabulated in this
  paper. The pressure range of the underlying Cu shock data (McQueen et al., 1970; Nellis
  et al., 1988) exceeds that of the Be static experiments and provides a high-pressure
  reference EOS for Cu.
doi: 10.1103/PhysRevB.65.172107

notes: >
  This YAML captures the AP2 EOS parameters for Cu (K0 = 133 GPa, K0' = 5.4) as used by
  Velisavljević et al. (2002) for pressure calibration. V0 is taken from standard ambient fcc
  Cu (0.011815 nm^3 per atom) consistent with later AP2-based Cu EOS work, though it is not
  explicitly refit in this study.

doi: 10.1103/PhysRevB.36.9790
notes: |
    This paper investigates the behavior of rhenium under extremely high pressures (up to 216 GPa) using energy-dispersive x-ray diffraction in a diamond-anvil cell.Their results show that rhenium maintains its hexagonal close-packed (hcp) structure throughout the entire range, with no phase transition observed, and that its axial ratio remains essentially constant with pressure. Additionally, the study presents one of the first x-ray-based pressure profiles across a diamond surface, revealing how pressure is distributed within the sample. Overall, the findings demonstrate both the structural stability of rhenium at extreme conditions and its suitability for use in high-pressure experimental setups.

Wei et al., 2024_Al2O3.yaml:
# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Wei et al., 2024
category: 1
composition: Al2O3  # ruby (Cr-doped α-Al2O3)
method: Luminescence
technique: DAC
DAC_type: Bx90
culet: 300 #um
cal_to_name: Datchi et al., 2007
cal_to_composition: cBN
eos: Power #P = (A0/B0) * [ (λ/λT)^B0 – 1]
A: 1881.16  # GPa
A_unc: 0.61
B: 10.94
B_unc: 0.03
lambda_0: 694.25  # nm
max_pressure: 58.6  # GPa
PTM: Ar
gasket: Re
synchrotron: SECUF #Synergic Extreme Condition User Facility
peaks_used_to_fit_sample: R1 and R2 luminescence 
reference_temperature: 296-850 #K
sample_heating: 
data_quality_notes: | 
    The ruby fluorescence dataset shows high signal quality and well-resolved R1 and R2 peaks at room temperature, but data quality degrades with increasing temperature due to thermal effects . Peak broadening, reduced intensity, and fluorescence quenching become significant above ~773–850 K, limiting measurement precision and eventually making ruby unusable at higher temperatures. Additionally, strong temperature-induced redshifts and pressure–temperature coupling introduce systematic errors if not corrected, with deviations increasing across the full P–T range. Even under quasi-hydrostatic conditions with Ar as a pressure-transmitting medium, nonhydrostatic effects may still contribute minor uncertainties. The use of cBN as an independent calibration standard improves reliability, but overall accuracy depends on applying temperature-corrected calibration relationships.
   
doi: 10.1063/5.0178597 
notes: | 
    This study calibrates ruby fluorescence as a pressure sensor up to 58.6 GPa and 850 K using a power-law relationship between pressure and wavelength shift, with parameters A = 1881.16 GPa and B = 10.94 at 296 K . The R1 and R2 luminescence lines are used to determine pressure, with calibration tied to a cBN reference scale. Ruby exhibits strong fluorescence intensity and clear spectral features at ambient conditions, making it a widely used pressure sensor. However, it shows significant temperature sensitivity, including peak broadening and redshift, requiring temperature-dependent corrections for accurate pressure determination. Compared to other sensors in the study, ruby has the strongest signal but the highest temperature sensitivity, limiting its effectiveness at high temperatures.

data_quality_notes: | 
    The ruby fluorescence pressure scale demonstrates strong signal intensity and well-defined R₁/R₂ peaks under ambient conditions, making it a reliable pressure calibrant in DAC experiments; however, its data quality degrades at elevated temperatures due to thermal broadening, reduced intensity, and significant redshift of the fluorescence lines . As shown in the spectra (e.g., Fig. 1 and Fig. 2), peak widths increase and signal-to-noise decreases with temperature, limiting precise measurements above ~773–850 K due to fluorescence quenching and peak overlap. Additionally, pressure calibration accuracy is affected by temperature–pressure coupling, meaning that neglecting temperature corrections can introduce systematic errors of several GPa at high P–T conditions. Calibration consistency also depends on the chosen ruby scale and pressure-transmitting medium, with non-hydrostatic effects (e.g., Ar vs He) introducing measurable deviations.
   
doi: 10.1063/5.0178597 
notes: |
    Ruby (Cr³⁺-doped Al₂O₃) is one of the most widely used optical pressure sensors in DAC experiments due to its strong fluorescence and clear pressure-dependent spectral shifts, particularly the R₁ line arising from crystal field splitting of Cr³⁺ ions . Its pressure calibration is well established up to tens of GPa and can be extended further with modern scales, making it a standard reference for high-pressure studies. However, the material exhibits strong temperature sensitivity, with fluorescence peaks shifting and broadening significantly at elevated temperatures, requiring temperature-corrected calibration models. Compared to newer sensors, ruby offers excellent signal strength but reduced reliability under high-temperature conditions, where alternative materials may provide better spectral stability.

    The Sm³⁺:YAG fluorescence sensor (particularly the Y₁ line) exhibits generally high data quality with relatively stable peak positions and lower temperature sensitivity compared to ruby, but several factors still limit precision under extreme conditions . As shown in the spectra and temperature trends (e.g., Fig. 6 and Fig. 9), peak broadening and intensity reduction occur at elevated temperatures, and line overlap (especially between Y₁ and Y₂) can complicate peak identification at high P–T conditions. Although temperature effects are smaller than in ruby, they are not negligible—ignoring them can introduce pressure errors of several GPa at high temperatures. Data consistency also depends on calibration choice and reference standards, with differences in prior studies attributed to synthesis variations, fitting methods (linear vs nonlinear), and pressure scales. Sm³⁺-doped yttrium aluminum garnet (YAG) is an optical pressure sensor that uses luminescence peak shifts (notably the Y₁ transition) to determine pressure in high-pressure experiments . It is valued for its lower temperature sensitivity compared to ruby and its ability to operate over a wider temperature range, making it suitable for high-temperature DAC studies. The Y₁ line shows a predictable pressure-dependent shift and becomes more sensitive than ruby at higher pressures (above ~30 GPa), improving its usefulness in extreme conditions.

data_quality_notes: | 
    The Y₂ fluorescence line of Sm³⁺:YAG provides generally reliable pressure measurements with good signal persistence at high pressures, but its data quality is influenced by temperature effects, peak evolution, and experimental conditions . Compared to Y₁, the Y₂ peak becomes increasingly dominant at higher pressures (surpassing Y₁ around ~40 GPa) and can remain observable to even higher pressures, which supports its usefulness in extreme regimes. However, temperature-induced peak shifts and broadening are still present, and neglecting temperature corrections can introduce multi-GPa errors under high P–T conditions. Additionally, at elevated temperatures, overlapping features and changes in relative peak intensity complicate accurate peak fitting. While non-hydrostatic effects exist, they are smaller than those affecting ruby, contributing to improved consistency. Variations across studies are also linked to calibration methods and sample differences. Overall, the Y₂ line maintains good usability at high pressure but requires careful spectral analysis and temperature-corrected calibration for high-precision work.
doi: 10.1063/5.0178597 
notes: | 
    Other fits are prospoed in this study we have selected equation 10 and table 4. The Y₂ luminescence line of Sm³⁺:YAG is an important pressure-sensitive feature used in DAC experiments, particularly at higher pressures where it becomes stronger and more stable than the Y₁ line . It shows a clear and predictable pressure-dependent wavelength shift, enabling reliable pressure calibration across a wide range. Compared to ruby, Sm³⁺:YAG (including Y₂) exhibits lower temperature sensitivity and better performance in high-temperature environments. The Y₂ line is especially valuable at extreme pressures due to its persistence and increasing intensity, making it suitable for extending measurements beyond the limits of other sensors. Although temperature effects are smaller, they are still significant enough to require correction for accurate results.

# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated February  2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Yamazaki et al., 2012
category: 1
composition: Fe-hcp
method: XRD
technique: Kawai-type multi-anvil press + synchrotron XRD
DAC_type: Sintered diamond Kawai-type apparatus
culet: 1.0 mm TEL

cal_to_name: Tsuchiya 2003
cal_to_composition: Au

eos: Rydberg-Vinet
order: 3

V0: 22.17   # Å^3/unit cell
V0_unc: 0.06

K0: 196   # GPa
is_K0_fixed: no
K0_unc: 8

K0_prime: 4.8
K0_prime_unc: 0.2

#gamma0: 3.2
#gamma0_unc: 0.2

#theta0: 1168   # K
#theta0_unc: 61

#q: 0.8
#q_unc: 0.3

max_pressure: 80   # GPa

PTM: MgO + 5% Cr2O3
synchrotron: SPring-8 BL04B1

peaks_used_to_fit_sample: >
  hcp Fe (100), (002), (101), (102), (110),
  (103), (112), (201)

reference_temperature: 300   # K

sample_heating: >
  Resistive heating using TiB2 + BN + AlN heater in a
  Kawai-type multianvil apparatus. Measurements performed
  up to 1900 K. Thermal pressure modeled using a
  Mie-Gruneisen-Debye formalism including quasi-harmonic,
  anharmonic, and electronic thermal pressure terms.

data_quality_notes: >
  In situ synchrotron XRD measurements performed on epsilon-Fe
  up to 80 GPa and 1900 K. Pressure determined from Au using
  the Tsuchiya (2003) Au EOS. The Vinet and third-order
  Birch-Murnaghan fits produced nearly identical compression
  curves over the measured pressure range.
  Authors report the hcp phase remained stable
  throughout the measured high-pressure range.

doi: 10.1029/2012GL053540

notes: |
  Table 1 reports both Birch-Murnaghan and Vinet EOS fits.
  This yaml corresponds to the Vinet fit:

    V0 = 22.17(6) Å^3
    KT0 = 196(8) GPa
    KT0' = 4.8(2)
    gamma0 = 3.2(2)
    theta0 = 1168(61) K
    q = 0.8(3)

  Pressure was determined from Au diffraction peaks using
  the Au EOS of Tsuchiya (2003).

  The thermal EOS was modeled using:

    P(V,T) = PT0(V) + ΔPth(V,T)

  where PT0(V) was fit using both:
    - third-order Birch-Murnaghan EOS
    - Vinet EOS

  The authors note that both EOS forms produce nearly
  identical compression curves within the experimental range.

  Measurements were conducted at:
    - pressures up to 80 GPa
    - temperatures up to 1900 K
    - using the SPEED-Mk.II Kawai apparatus at SPring-8.

  together with MgO, Pt, Au, Ne, and ruby under the Ye et al. (2018) framework.

  Ruby scale refit based on simultaneous compression of MgO and ruby in He.
  Parameters A = 1887(3) GPa and m = 5.4(1) match Dorogokupets & Oganov (2007)
  within 0.5 GPa to 150 GPa. This scale is internally consistent with MgO-Y17,
  Pt-Y17, Au-Y17, Ne, and NaCl-B2 calibrations identified in this study.

notes: |
  This ruby scale is not the Mao (1986) or Piermarini (1975) form. Instead, it is a 
  refit of the Aleksandrov et al. (1987) / Dorogokupets–Oganov polynomial expression:

  This scale is recommended for pressure determination when using the internally
  consistent calibrant set MgO–Pt–Au–Ne–NaCl-B2 defined in Ye et al. (2018).

ata_quality_notes: |
    The data remeasured shock compression data up to ~550–580 GPa, obtained with modern experimental techniques (e.g., two-stage light-gas gun and improved velocity measurements), which significantly reduce uncertainties compared to older datasets . Reported uncertainties in shock velocity are ≤2%, leading to pressure uncertainties of ~2% up to ~400 GPa. A key strength is that the EOS is constructed entirely from pressure-scale-independent (primary) data, avoiding bias from secondary calibrants. However, shock experiments inherently probe high-temperature Hugoniot states rather than isothermal conditions, requiring model-dependent corrections (e.g., Mie–Grüneisen framework and thermal contributions), which introduce systematic uncertainty. Additional limitations include reliance on fitting procedures (least-squares optimization) and sensitivity to the Hugoniot data, which is identified as the dominant source of uncertainty, particularly at extreme pressures and temperatures.
doi: 10.1103/PhysRevB.80.104114 
notes: | 
    Yokoo et al. (2009) present a revised primary equation of state for gold using shock compression data up to ~550 GPa, incorporating thermal and electronic contributions within a Mie–Grüneisen framework . The EOS is constrained using both high-pressure shock data and independent ambient-pressure thermodynamic properties (e.g., thermal expansion, bulk modulus, and heat capacity), resulting in a self-consistent model. Compared to earlier studies, the revised EOS yields higher pressures, correcting systematic underestimations from older shock datasets. The model demonstrates excellent agreement with experimental Hugoniot data and thermodynamic measurements, with uncertainties typically within ~2%. Gold is reaffirmed as a reliable primary pressure standard over a broad P–T range, and consistency with a similarly derived platinum EOS further supports the approach.

data_quality_notes: | 
    Shock compression data extending to ~550–660 GPa, combined with independent thermodynamic constraints, allowing construction of a primary (scale-free) pressure standard . Uncertainties are largely controlled by the accuracy of the shock Hugoniot, with pressure errors estimated to be on the order of a few percent, and similar to those for gold. A key strength is the consistent modeling approach using the same framework as Au, which enables cross-validation between the two materials. However, as with all shock-based EOS, the data represent high-temperature Hugoniot states rather than isothermal conditions, requiring model-dependent thermal corrections (e.g., phonon and electronic contributions), which introduce systematic uncertainty. Additional uncertainty arises from fitting procedures and parameter correlations, particularly in determining K0 and K0 prime.
doi: 10.1103/PhysRevB.80.104114
notes: | 
    Yokoo et al. (2009) determine a revised equation of state for platinum using shock compression data combined with ambient-pressure thermodynamic measurements, applying a Mie–Grüneisen framework with a third-order formulation . The resulting EOS uses V0 = 15.1199 A^3 per atom, K0 = 276.4 GPa, and K0 prime ≈ 5.48, and extends reliably to ~550 GPa. Compared to previous Pt scales, this model yields slightly lower pressures (by up to ~3%), improving agreement with independent datasets. The Pt EOS is shown to be highly consistent with the revised Au EOS, with differences of only ~1–3% across a wide pressure range, reinforcing both as reliable primary pressure standards.
