data_quality_notes: |
  Zha et al. (2000) derive a primary pressure–density scale for MgO to 55 GPa
  by integrating single-crystal Brillouin velocities with polycrystalline x-ray
  diffraction densities under nearly hydrostatic He conditions. Using this MgO
  primary scale, they recalibrate the ruby R1 fluorescence pressure scale and
  fit Eq. (7): P(GPa) = (A/B)*[(λ/λ0)^B - 1], with A fixed at 1904 GPa and
  B = 7.715. This functional form is identical to the Mao et al. (1986) ruby
  scale but with slightly larger curvature B, reflecting the more hydrostatic
  MgO-based primary calibration. The authors state that the resulting ruby
  scale is accurate to within ~1% up to 55 GPa under hydrostatic conditions.

doi: 10.1073/pnas.240466697
notes: |
  This YAML records the MgO-based ruby scale from Zha et al. (2000), which
  refines the curvature parameter B in the standard P = (A/B)[(λ/λ0)^B – 1]
  form. The initial slope A = 1904 GPa is retained from earlier ruby scales,
  while B = 7.715 is determined by fitting ruby shifts to the MgO primary
  pressure–density relation. λ0 is taken as the conventional ambient R1
  wavelength (~694.2 nm) consistent with Piermarini et al. (1975) and
  subsequent ruby work.

data_quality_notes: |
  Zha et al. (2000) determine a primary pressure–density equation of state for MgO
  to 55 GPa by combining single-crystal Brillouin scattering (elastic tensor C11,
  C12, C44) with polycrystalline x-ray diffraction densities under nearly hydrostatic
  helium conditions. The isothermal bulk modulus and its pressure derivative at 300 K
  are K0T = 160.2(7) GPa and K0T' = 4.03(3), respectively, obtained by converting the
  adiabatic moduli to isothermal and integrating KT = ρ (dP/dρ)T. The reference density
  is ρ0 = 3.585 g/cm^3 for MgO at 300 K, from which V0 ≈ 18.67 Å^3 per formula unit is
  derived. The authors estimate that the resulting MgO-based primary pressure scale
  has an accuracy of ~1% to 55 GPa under hydrostatic conditions.

  which is obtained by integrating Brillouin-derived elastic moduli with XRD densities
  and fitting the resulting pressure–density relation to a third-order Birch–Murnaghan
  form. The resulting MgO EOS is then used in the same paper to recalibrate the ruby
  fluorescence pressure scale, providing a hydrostatic primary reference for pressures
  up to 55 GPa.

data_quality_notes: |    
    The gold equation-of-state data derived from XRD in the toroidal DAC (tDAC) demonstrates generally high quality but is influenced by nonhydrostatic compression conditions and peak overlap at extreme pressures . Volumes were calculated using only the (111) diffraction peak, which is least sensitive to uniaxial stress, improving reliability compared to multi-peak approaches; however, residual stress effects are still present due to the absence of a pressure-transmitting medium. At pressures above ~275 GPa, overlap between Au and Pt peaks required fixing peak widths during fitting, introducing additional uncertainty in peak position determination. Despite these challenges, the Au P–V data show a smooth compression trend and agree within ~1% in volume with hydrostatic reference equations of state up to ~4 Mbar, indicating strong internal consistency. Pressure uncertainties propagate from calibration to the Pt standard (Fratanduono et al., 2021), and residuals of up to ~4% relative to this scale are observed at the highest pressures. Overall, the dataset is robust for ultrahigh-pressure EOS fitting, but accuracy is limited by nonhydrostatic stress, peak overlap, and dependence on external calibration.
doi: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-024-61861-2] #
notes: | 
    This study uses synchrotron XRD measurements of gold in a toroidal DAC to constrain its equation of state up to ~275–414 GPa, applying a third-order Rydberg–Vinet model with fixed V0, K0, and a fitted K0' of ~6.05 . Gold is used as a secondary material calibrated against a platinum pressure scale, enabling cross-validation of pressure at multi-megabar conditions. The use of the (111) lattice plane provides a closer approximation to hydrostatic compression under nonhydrostatic conditions. Results show that Au compression follows a smooth trend and matches previous hydrostatic and dynamic EOS studies, supporting its reliability as a reference material. The work also demonstrates that large-culet toroidal DACs can generate high-quality Au data at pressures exceeding 4 Mbar, extending static compression capabilities.

data_quality_notes: | 
    The gold EOS dataset extending to 414 GPa maintains overall good quality but is more affected by high-pressure limitations compared to lower-pressure fits . Volumes are derived from the (111) diffraction peak to reduce sensitivity to uniaxial stress under nonhydrostatic conditions, though the absence of a pressure-transmitting medium still introduces residual stress. Above ~275 GPa, significant peak overlap between Au and Pt requires fixing peak widths during fitting, which increases uncertainty in peak position and volume determination. Despite this, the compression trend remains smooth, and the data are consistent with lower-pressure fits and prior studies. Agreement with hydrostatic EOS references is within ~1% in volume, though pressure residuals can reach up to ~4% at the highest pressures. Calibration to the Pt standard (Fratanduono et al., 2021) remains a key source of systematic uncertainty.
doi:  [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-024-61861-2]
notes: | 
    This study determines the equation of state of gold up to 414 GPa using synchrotron XRD in a DAC, applying a third-order Rydberg-Vinet model with V0 = 16.962 A^3 per atom, K0 = 157 GPa, and K0 prime = 6.35 . Pressure is calibrated using platinum based on the Fratanduono et al. (2021) scale, and the (111) lattice plane is used to approximate hydrostatic compression behavior. The results extend Au EOS constraints into the multi-megabar regime and remain consistent with both lower-pressure fits and previous hydrostatic and dynamic studies. Allowing K0 to vary enables fitting across the full pressure range, including regions affected by peak overlap.

data_quality_notes: | 
    The rhenium EOS dataset obtained from XRD in the DAC shows consistent compression behavior up to 414 GPa, though it is influenced by nonhydrostatic conditions and calibration dependence . Volumes are derived from multiple lattice planes ((100), (101), (102)), improving robustness compared to single-peak approaches, but residual uniaxial stress is present due to the absence of a pressure-transmitting medium. The pressure calibration relies on the Pt standard, introducing systematic uncertainty tied to that EOS. Despite these factors, the Re P–V data display a smooth trend and agree closely (within ~0.75% in volume) with hydrostatic reference equations of state, indicating high internal consistency. No major peak overlap issues are reported for Re, supporting more stable fitting at ultrahigh pressures compared to Au. Overall, the dataset is reliable for EOS determination at multi-megabar conditions, with remaining uncertainties primarily from nonhydrostatic stress and calibration transfer.
doi:  [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-024-61861-2]
notes: | 
    This study determines the equation of state of rhenium up to 414 GPa using synchrotron XRD in a DAC, applying a third-order Rydberg-Vinet model with V0 = 14.733 A^3 per atom, K0 = 360 GPa (fixed), and K0 prime = 4.73 . Pressure is calibrated using platinum based on the Fratanduono et al. (2021) scale, and multiple diffraction peaks are used to determine volume. The results show a smooth compression curve that agrees well with previous hydrostatic and theoretical studies, particularly those using helium as a pressure medium. Fixing K0 based on ultrasonic data improves stability of the fit while allowing K0 prime to capture high-pressure behavior. Overall, rhenium is confirmed as a reliable material for high-pressure EOS studies and as a potential calibration reference at extreme conditions.

# This is a yaml file based on data published in the study listed below. All values listed are based on what can be found within the publication.This document was updated July 2026, any changes to the manuscript after this date have not been added.
study: Zurkowski et al., 2026
category: 1
composition: Ta
method: XRD 
technique: tDAC

cal_to_name: Fratanduono et al., 2020; Campbell et al., 2023
cal_to_composition: Cu; Bi 

eos: Rydberg-Vinet
order: 3

V0_per_atom: 18.037   # Å^3/atom
V0_unc: 0.002

K0: 172      
is_K0_fixed: no
K0_unc: 1
K0_prime: 4.43
K0_prime_unc: 0.04

max_pressure: 433 
PTM: none, Cu, Bi, Ne

synchrotron: 16-ID-B (APS, HPCAT) and PETRA-III (P02.2, DESY)

reference_temperature: 298   # K

sample_heating: >
  No heating

data_quality_notes: >
  The authors tested this third-order Vinet fit but did not use it as their preferred EoS.
  They report that the pressure residuals increase substantially above 300 GPa, and that the
  third-order fit does not reproduce the ultrasonically derived ambient isothermal bulk modulus
  within uncertainty. The fourth-order Vinet fit is preferred.

notes: |
  The abstract "The volume compression of body-centered cubic tantalum has been measured to 433 in the standard bevel and toroidal diamond anvil cell (DAC) using synchrotron-based angle-dispersive powder x-ray diffraction. Six experiments were carried out achieving a maximum pressure of 433(6) GPa. Pressures were estimated using an equation of state (EoS) of Cu or Bi for all experiments. We additionally calculated the elastic constants of Ta and Bi and used our previously calculated values for Cu to inform the stress state of the sample and an EoS of Ta for comparison to our experimental data. Our experimental pressure–volume data for Ta was fit with a fourth-order Vinet EoS, resulting in parameters K0 = 192(5) GPa, η  = 6.4(6), β = −35(5), and Ψ  = 141(13). This Ta EoS is consistent within uncertainty with the ultrasonically derived ambient isothermal bulk modulus, with existing DAC data up to 100 GPa, and with Ta ramp EoS measurements that exceed the pressures of the current study."

data_quality_notes: >
  For the Cu and Bi pressure-medium experiments, Ta volumes from individual lattice planes agree within about 1%, which they interpret as evidence for a more uniform compression environment. In contrast, the no-PTM tDAC data show larger nonsystematic mismatch between lattice-plane-derived volumes, especially above ~200 GPa. They interpret this as a stress-state effect, where different Ta lattice planes respond differently to deviatoric stress.

In Fig. 4a, the residuals relative to the fourth-order Vinet fit remain within ~9 GPa across the full pressure range. They also note that the residuals do not show a clear systematic dependence on pressure medium, which supports their claim that Cu, Bi, Ne, and no-PTM experiments produce broadly consistent bulk compression behavior.
